SFIREG WILL HOLD FOUR IN-PERSON MEETINGS EACH CALENDAR YEAR (2 FULL SFIREG MEETINGS; 2 JOINT WORKING COMMITTEE MEETINGS). DESCRIPTION:INSECT PESTS REDUCE PLANT HEALTH AND MARKETABILITY, WHICH FREQUENTLY REQUIRES CHEMICAL INTERVENTION TO PRODUCE A SALEABLE PRODUCT. POLLINATORS PLAY A CRITICAL ROLE IN U.S. AGRICULTURE AND ECOSYSTEM FUNCTIONING, BUT INSECTICIDES USED DURING CROP PRODUCTION CAN HARM THEM. MOST POLLINATOR TOXICOLOGICAL DATA FOCUS ON BEES. LEPIDOPTERAN POLLINATORS (I.E., BUTTERFLIES AND MOTHS) DIFFER IN SUSCEPTIBILITY TO INSECTICIDES COMPARED TO BEES AND HAVE DIFFERENT ROUTES OF EXPOSURE (E.G., LARVAL LEAF-FEEDING VERSUS NECTAR CONSUMPTION). CONSEQUENTLY, PRODUCTS COMPATIBLE WITH BEE CONSERVATION POSE AN UNKNOWN RISK TO LEPIDOPTERAN POLLINATORS, YET BOTH TAXA ARE PERVASIVE IN AGROECOSYSTEMS. FLORIDA'S ORNAMENTAL PLANT INDUSTRY HAS AN ESTIMATED ECONOMIC IMPACT OF $21 BILLION, RANKING SECOND IN THE NATION. ORNAMENTAL PLANTS ARE A MAJOR COMPONENT OF RAPIDLY EXPANDING URBAN LANDSCAPES WHERE 80% OF THE U.S. POPULATION RESIDES, THUS MAKING PESTICIDE USE DURING PRODUCTION AND PERENNIAL MAINTENANCE RELEVANT TO MOST PEOPLE AND TO FUTURE LANDSCAPES. WE WILL DEVELOP LEPIDOPTERA IPPM STRATEGIES FOR ORNAMENTAL PLANT PRODUCTION, USE OUR RESULTS TO CREATE A WILDLIFE-FRIENDLY PLANT PRODUCTION CERTIFICATION PROGRAM, AND INCREASE THE POSITIVE ENVIRONMENTAL AND ECOLOGICAL IMPACT OF ORNAMENTAL PLANT PRODUCTION.ACTIVITIES:1. REVIEW NOVEL DATA DOCUMENTING THE ACUTE, CHRONIC, LETHAL, AND SUBLETHAL EFFECTS OF PESTICIDES TO MONARCHS OVER TIME.
2. PUBLISH IN TWO SCIENTIFIC PUBLICATIONS DISCUSSING PROJECT RESULTS AND IMPLICATIONS SUBMITTED TO AN OPEN-SOURCE, PEER REVIEWED JOURNALS.
3. DISTILL AND REVIEW ANY NEW EVIDENCE-BASED IPPM RECOMMENDATIONS FOR CONTROLLING KEY SAP-FEEDING INSECT PESTS WHILE MITIGATING IMPACTS TO LEPIDOPTERAN POLLINATORS IN A PLANT PRODUCTION ENVIRONMENT.
4. DEVELOP A COST-BENEFIT ANALYSIS TO DETERMINE ECONOMIC VIABILITY AND EFFECTIVENESS OF IPPM RECOMMENDATIONS.
5. CONCEPTUALIZE AND PRODUCE FIVE WEB-BASED TRAINING MODULES TO CERTIFY INDUSTRY PROFESSIONALS IN THE USE OF IPPM STRATEGIES TO PROTECT INSECT POLLINATORS DURING ORNAMENTAL PLANT PRODUCTION.
6. PUBLISH IN TWO INDUSTRY TRADE JOURNAL ARTICLES AND TWO EXTENSION PUBLICATIONS DISSEMINATING OUR IPPM RECOMMENDATIONS.
7. PARTICIPATE IN ONE LOCAL, ONE STATEWIDE, ONE REGIONAL, AND ONE NATIONAL TRAINING EVENT TO INCREASE AWARENESS OF IPPM PRODUCTION PRACTICES.
8. DEVELOP FINAL REPORT SUMMARIZING ALL PROJECT ACTIVITIES, DATA, PUBLICATIONS, AND QUANTIFIED OUTCOMES.SUBRECIPIENT:THE APPLICANT HAS BUDGETED A SUBCONTRACT TO UF ICBR INSECTICIDE RESIDUE ANALYSIS. IN ADDITION TO QUANTIFYING EFFECTS OF INSECTICIDES ON MONARCHS VIA EXPOSURE THROUGH HERBIVORY,THE APPLICANT WILL QUANTIFY POTENTIAL EXPOSURE TO BUTTERFLIES AND OTHER POLLINATORS VIA THE MOVEMENT OF INSECTICIDES INTO MILKWEED NECTAR. TROPICAL MILKWEED FLOWERS ARE ACTIVELY VISITED BY BEES AND BUTTERFLIES, AND EVIDENCE SUGGESTS THAT SOME SYSTEMIC INSECTICIDES CAN REDUCE MONARCH ADULT LONGEVITY VIA NECTAR FEEDING AND THUS MAY SERVE AS ANOTHER ROUTE FOR NON-TARGET INSECTICIDE IMPACTS (JAMES 2019). WE WILL OBTAIN A. CURASSAVICA PLANTS (12 PER INSECTICIDE TREATMENT) THAT ARE IN FULL BLOOM, PRUNE OFF ALL FLOWERS, MAKE FOLIAR INSECTICIDE APPLICATIONS, AND MAINTAIN THE PLANTS USING STANDARD PRODUCTION PRACTICES TO PROMOTE NEW FLOWER DEVELOPMENT. DESCRIPTION:THE OVERALL GOAL OF THE PROPOSED WORK IS TO INCREASE GROWER ADOPTION OF INTEGRATED PEST AND POLLINATOR MANAGEMENT TACTICS IN WATERMELON PRODUCTION. THROUGH A STATEWIDE SCOUTING PROGRAM, THE GRANTEE WILL PARTNER WITH ILLINOIS AND INDIANA GROWERS TO ADDRESS THE CHALLENGES IMPLEMENTING IPPM PRACTICES ON THEIR FARMS. IN TOTAL, THE GRANTEE EXPECTS THIS PROJECT TO IMPACT OVER 200 WATERMELON GROWERS AND THE 6,000 ACRES OF FARMLAND THEY COLLECTIVELY MANAGE. FUNDS WILL BE USED TO SUPPORT A GRADUATE STUDENT TO CONDUCT ON-FARM DEMONSTRATION TRIALS DURING THE SUMMERS OF 2022 AND 2023. IN ACCOMPLISHING THESE GOALS, THE GRANTEE ANTICIPATE REDUCING INSECTICIDE/MITICIDE APPLICATIONS IN SEEDLESS WATERMELON PRODUCTION BY 30% (2-3 SPRAYS PER SEASON) COMPARED TO CURRENT REGIMES, WHICH WILL HAVE A NUMBER OF SHORT- AND LONG-TERM BENEFITS TO GROWERS. THE GRANTEE EXPECTS DECREASED INSECTICIDE APPLICATION, INCREASED POLLINATION, AND IMPROVED FRUIT QUALITY WITH INCREASED IPPM ADOPTION IN THE SHORT-TERM. IN THE LONG-TERM, THE GRANTEE ANTICIPATES GREATER ACCESS TO PRIME MARKETS (I.E., WAL-MART'S NEW POLICY MANDATING ADOPTION OF IPM AND 'BEE-SAFE PRACTICES' BY 2025) DUE TO DECREASED PESTICIDE RESIDUES AND INCREASED VIABILITY OF AGROECOSYSTEMS THROUGH A REDUCTION IN OFF-TARGET EFFECTS FROM INSECTICIDE USE.ACTIVITIES:1. GRANTEE WILL IMPLEMENT A THRESHOLD-BASED SCOUTING PROGRAM TO INCREASE GROWER ADOPTION OF INTEGRATED PEST AND POLLINATOR MANAGEMENT PRACTICES.
A: GRANTEE CREATED AN IPPM PROGRAM WITH THE PRIMARY GOAL OF INCREASING THESE PRACTICES IN SEEDLESS WATERMELON PRODUCTION. THIS WILL REQUIRE INTENSIVE COLLABORATIONS WITH GROWERS, IN WHICH GRANTEE WILL SCOUT GROWER FIELDS AND PROVIDE WEEKLY REPORTS AND RECOMMENDATIONS FOR PRIMARY ARTHROPOD PESTS. EACH PARTICIPATING GROWER WILL SELECT ONE 20-30 ACRE WATERMELON FIELD TO BE SCOUTED WEEKLY. PLANTS WILL BE INSPECTED FOR THE PRESENCE OF COMMON ARTHROPOD PESTS. MOST PESTS WILL BE SAMPLED ON A PER PLANT BASIS (E.G., STRIPED CUCUMBER BEETLES, APHIDS, SQUASH BUGS); HOWEVER, SOME WILL BE ON A PER FLOWER OR PER LEAF BASIS. MITES WILL BE COUNTED ON THREE LEAVES PER PLANT USING A LOUPE (~240 LEAVES PER FIELD). PICKLEWORM LARVAE WILL BE SAMPLED BY PULLING 4 MALE FLOWERS PER PLANT AND EXAMINING EACH FOR ENTRY HOLES (~320 FLOWERS PER FIELD). AT THE END OF EACH GROWING SEASON, GRANTEE WILL REQUEST THAT GROWERS SUBMIT THEIR PESTICIDE RECORDS OF FIELDS SAMPLED BY THE SCOUT (I.E., PRODUCTS, RATES, DATES OF APPLICATION). PESTICIDE APPLICATION RECORDS WILL BE COMPARED WITH WEEKLY SCOUTING DATA TO DETERMINE WHETHER THE GROWER COMPLIED WITH OUR IPPM GUIDELINES (I.E., FOLLOWING THE THRESHOLD AND/OR APPLYING PRODUCTS WITH REDUCED BEE TOXICITY VALUES). GRANTEE WILL MEET WITH ALL GROWERS ON AN ANNUAL BASIS TO DISCUSS INSECTICIDE RECORDS, IN ADDITION TO WHAT STRATEGIES EITHER WORKED OR DID NOT WORK TO MANAGE THE TARGET ARTHROPOD PESTS. GROWERS WILL ALSO BE REQUIRED TO SUBMIT A SURVEY IN WHICH THEY DESCRIBE THEIR EXPERIENCES EITHER FOLLOWING OR FORGOING THE IPPM RECOMMENDATIONS.

2. GRANTEE WILL EVALUATE THE IMPACT OF IPPM PRACTICES ON WATERMELON QUALITY (POLLINATION AND INSECTICIDE RESIDUES).
A: GRANTEE WILL LEVERAGE DATA COLLECTED FROM OBJECTIVE 1. GRANTEE WILL PAIR POLLINATION OBSERVATIONS, YIELD ESTIMATES, AND RESIDUE SAMPLES TO CONDUCT POST-HOC ANALYSES AIMED AT CORRELATING THE ADOPTION OF IPPM PRACTICES WITH WATERMELON MARKETABILITY OUTCOMES. GRANTEE WILL CONDUCT POLLINATION OBSERVATIONS IN ALL TREATMENT FIELDS DURING THE BLOOM PERIOD. ALL 10 FARMS WILL BE VISITED BIWEEKLY OVER A FOUR-WEEK PERIOD (2 OBSERVATIONS PER FARM). GRANTEE WILL CONDUCT YIELD ASSESSMENTS AT TWO POINTS DURING THE GROWING SEASON, DURING BLOOM AND ONE WEEK BEFORE HARVEST. THE SIZE OF ALL MELONS WILL BE MEASURED (LENGTH AND WIDTH OF MELONS) USING A MEASURING TAPE WITHIN SAMPLING AREAS, 2M TIMES; 2M. THIS WILL BE REPLICATED 40 TIMES IN EACH FIELD. GRANTEE WILL HARVEST 20 MELONS FROM EACH FIELD TO EXTRACT INSECTICIDE RESIDUES

DESCRIPTION:THE PURPOSE OF THIS PROPOSAL IS TO STRENGTHEN SUSTAINABLE PEST MANAGEMENT IN THE PACIFIC NORTHWEST (PNW) BY SLOWING THE DEVELOPMENT OF HERBICIDE RESISTANT WEEDS, PRESERVING THE USEFUL LIFE OF CURRENT HERBICIDE PRODUCTS, AND IMPROVING ADOPTION OF EXISTING INTEGRATED PEST MANAGEMENT (IPM) TOOLS. WE WILL ACCOMPLISH THESE GOALS BY TRAINING AGRICULTURAL PROFESSIONALS ON HERBICIDE RESISTANCE PLANNING, MANAGEMENT, AND REMEDIATION, THUS EMPOWERING THESE PROFESSIONALS TO TRANSFER AND AMPLIFY THE SKILLS AND KNOWLEDGE GAINED IN OUR WORKSHOPS TO GROWERS. THIS PROJECT WILL BE BROADLY APPLICABLE TO THE DIVERSITY OF CONVENTIONALLY-PRODUCED FIELD CROP OPERATIONS ON 9.7 MILLION ACRES OF IRRIGATED AND NON-IRRIGATED LAND IN THE PNW, WILL SERVE AS A TEMPLATE FOR PROGRAMS IN OTHER REGIONS OF THE UNITED STATES, AND FOR FUTURE IPM RESISTANCE PROGRAMS TARGETING INSECTS AND DISEASES IN THE PNW.ACTIVITIES:1. YEAR 1: APPLICANT WILL DEVELOP CURRICULA FOR UP TO FOUR ONE-HOUR, WEB-BASED, ON-DEMAND TRAINING MODULES BROADLY APPLICABLE TO THE DIVERSITY OF CONVENTIONALLY-PRODUCED FIELD CROP OPERATIONS ON 9.7 MILLION ACRES OF IRRIGATED AND NON-IRRIGATED LAND IN THE PACIFIC NORTH WEST. ONLINE, INTERACTIVE TRAINING MODULES WILL BE AVAILABLE IN ENGLISH AND SPANISH. APPLICANT WILL DEVELOP TRAINING MATERIALS ON HERBICIDE RESISTANCE MANAGEMENT PLANS, BEST MANAGEMENT PRACTICES, AND REMEDIAL ACTION PLANTS. INFORMATION WILL BE INCLUDED IN ONLINE TRAINING MODULES.
2. YEAR 2: APPLICANT WILL HOST THREE REGION-SPECIFIC, IN-PERSON WORKSHOPS FOR STAKEHOLDERS REPRESENTING OVER 1.58 MILLION ACRES OF AGRICULTURAL LAND IN OREGON'S WILLAMETTE VALLEY, 8 MILLION ACRES OF AGRICULTURAL LAND IN THE COLUMBIA BASIN OF OREGON AND WASHINGTON, AND 387,000 ACRES OF UNDER-REPRESENTED AGRICULTURAL LAND IN THE TREASURE VALLEY OF OREGON AND IDAHO. 1. UP TO FOUR ONE-HOUR WEB-BASED TRAINING MODULES ON HERBICIDE RESISTANCE IN PNW SYSTEMS, HOSTED BY OSU'S PACE PLATFORM.
2.THREE REGION-SPECIFIC, IN-PERSON WORKSHOPS ON HERBICIDE RESISTANCE DELIVERED AT OSU'S RESEARCH AND EXPERIMENTS CENTERS TO COVER CROPPING SYSTEMS IN THE WILLAMETTE VALLEY, THE COLUMBIA BASIN OF OREGON AND WASHINGTON, AND THE TREASURE VALLEY OF OREGON AND IDAHO.
3. AN EASILY-ADAPTABLE, TIME-BASED TEACHING PLAN PROVIDING LEARNING OBJECTIVES, LESSON PLANS, ACTIVITIES AND EVALUATION PLANS TO ASSIST WORKSHOP PARTICIPANTS IN TRANSFERRING HERBICIDE

DESCRIPTION:THIS ACTION APPROVES AN AWARD IN THE AMOUNT OF $55,261 TO GEORGIA DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE TO PROVIDE IMPLEMENT THE 'GEORGIA DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE (GDA) 2022 REGION 4 INTEGRATED PEST MANAGEMENT GRANT' PROJECT.  THIS AWARD WILL ALLOW THE GDA TO REDUCE UNNECESSARY EXPOSURES TO PESTS AND PESTICIDES THROUGH AN EDUCATION INITIATIVE EXPANDING INTEGRATED PEST MANAGEMENT TO ENCOURAGE SMART, SENSIBLE, AND SUSTAINABLE PEST CONTROL IN SCHOOLS AND HOMES. ACTIVITIES:TO REACH STUDENTS, GDA WILL CREATE A CURRICULUM ABOUT STRUCTURAL INTEGRATED PEST MANAGEMENT (IPM) FOR ELEMENTARY SCHOOL STUDENTS. TOPICS COVERED WILL INCLUDE PEST IDENTIFICATION, THE STEPS OF IPM, AND PESTICIDE SAFETY. GDA INSPECTORS WILL USE THIS CURRICULUM IN ELEMENTARY SCHOOL CLASSES ACROSS THE STATE. GDA WILL ALSO USE THE FUNDS TO SPONSOR SCHOOL PEST MANAGEMENT PROFESSIONALS TO ATTEND THE GDA SCHOOL IPM WORKSHOP AT NO COST TO THEM. THIS WILL ENABLE SCHOOL EMPLOYEES, WHO HAD BEEN PREVIOUSLY PROHIBITED BY THE COST OF THE WORKSHOP OR TRAVEL EXPENSES, TO ATTEND. EXPECTED OUTCOMES INCLUDE PROTECTION OF NON-PEST INSECT POPULATIONS, A REDUCTION IN PESTICIDE APPLICATIONS, LOWERED INCIDENCE OF PESTICIDE EXPOSURES, A REDUCTION IN PESTICIDE APPLICATIONS, AND A REDUCTION IN SCHOOL PEST MANAGEMENT COSTS. INTENDED BENEFICIARIES OF THE PROJECT INCLUDE THE STUDENTS AND PEST MANAGEMENT PROFESSIONALS OF ENVIRONMENTAL JUSTICE TARGET SCHOOLS, AS WELL AS TEACHERS AND THE PARENTS OF THOSE STUDENTS.

DESCRIPTION:THE GOAL OF THIS PROJECT IS TO REDUCE NEONICOTINOID SEED TREATMENTS (NSTS) APPLIED TO ROW CROPS IN VERMONT. THE OBJECTIVE IS TO PROVIDE FARMERS EDUCATION AND EXPERIENCE WITH SCOUTING AND ASSESSING ACTION THRESHOLDS FOR EARLY SEASON PESTS IN ROW CROPS. THROUGH THIS NEWLY GAINED KNOWLEDGE FARMERS WILL BE ABLE TO MAKE MORE INFORMED DECISIONS AS TO WHETHER A NST IS NEEDED TO PREVENT ANY CROP LOSSES. THE OUTPUTS OF THE PROJECT INCLUDE SIX ON-FARM DEMONSTRATIONS, 30 ONE-ON-ONE CONSULTATIONS, SIX ON-FARM FIELDS DAYS, ONE WEBINAR SERIES, TWO CROP SPECIFIC SCOUTING FACTSHEETS, AND EIGHT PRESENTATIONS TO GROWER AND STAKEHOLDER AUDIENCES. THE APPLICANT EXPECTS TO EDUCATE OVER 1000 NORTHEASTERN GROWERS ON IPM PRACTICES, PROVIDE 250 FARMERS WITH HANDS-ON TRAINING, AND REDUCE NSTS ON AT LEAST 1500 ACRES.ACTIVITIES:A VARIETY OF ACTIVITIES WILL OCCUR TO EDUCATE FARMERS ON HOW TO SCOUT FOR SOIL PESTS, EVALUATE THRESHOLDS, AND DETERMINE PEST MANAGEMENT ACTIONS IN CORN AND SOYBEAN FIELDS IN VERMONT. ULTIMATELY, HELPING FARMERS IMPLEMENT INTEGRATED PEST MANAGEMENT (IPM) PRACTICES TO AVOID SUPERFLUOUS NSTS IF THEY ARE NOT NECESSARY.

1. SIX PARTNER FARMS WILL SERVE AS DEMONSTRATION SITES WHERE SCOUTING, THRESHOLDS, AND ACTIONS ARE TAKEN TO DETERMINE IF PESTICIDES ARE WARRANTED. TEN ACRES OF CORN OR SOYBEANS WILL BE USED FOR THE DEMONSTRATION ON EACH FARM. HALF THE FIELD WILL USE THE FARMER'S CURRENT PRACTICES AND HALF OF THE  FIELD WILL USE IPM PRACTICES. THESE FIELDS WILL SERVE AS EDUCATIONAL SITES WHERE FARMERS AND OTHER AGRICULTURAL INDUSTRY STAKEHOLDERS CAN LEARN ABOUT IPM TACTICS FOR SOIL PESTS THAT IMPACT CORN AND SOYBEAN.
2. THIRTY PARTNER FARMS WILL BE ACTIVE PARTICIPANTS IN THE TWO-YEAR PROJECT TO GAIN DIRECT EXPERIENCE AND ONE-ON-ONE TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE WITH SCOUTING, ACTION THRESHOLDS, AND MANAGEMENT DECISIONS. THESE FARMS WILL WORK WITH PROJECT TEAM TO SELECT AT LEAST ONE FIELD TO SCOUT, RECEIVE SCOUTING
TOOLS, AND ASSISTANCE WITH SCOUTING AND ACTION THRESHOLDS. 
3. WEBINAR SERIES - A WINTER PEST MANAGEMENT WEBINAR SERIES WILL BE HELD IN JAN/FEB OF 2022. THE WEBINAR SERIES WILL HIGHLIGHT PESTS OF CORN AND SOYBEANS, SCOUTING AND MONITORING OF THESE PESTS, ACTION THRESHOLDS, AND PEST MANAGEMENT TOOLS. ENVIRONMENTAL CONCERNS RELATED TO PESTICIDES AND OVERUSE WILL ALSO BE COVERED.
4. ON-FARM FIELD DAYS - THREE FIELD DAYS PER YEAR HELD AT DEMONSTRATION FARMS WILL PROVIDE PARTICIPANTS WITH HANDS-ON TRAINING FOR SCOUTING, IDENTIFICATION, AND ACTION THRESHOLDS.
PRESENTATION AT LOCAL AND REGIONAL WINTER CONFERENCES - NO-TILL COVER CROP CONFERENCE, GRAIN CONFERENCE, DAIRY MEETINGS, AND CERTIFIED CROP ADVISOR CONFERENCES WILL SERVE AS A PLATFORM TO DELIVER PROJECT INFORMATION TO OUR TARGET AUDIENCE. IN 2022, AN IPM INTENSIVE WILL BE HELD AT THE ANNUAL NO-TILL COVER CROP CONFERENCE TO TRAIN STAKEHOLDERS AND IN 2023 A FARMER-TO-FARMER SESSION TO HIGHLIGHT IPM OUTCOMES. SCOUTING FACTSHEETS FOR CORN AND SOYBEAN - FACTSHEETS HIGHLIGHTING PROTOCOLS, ACTION THRESHOLDS, AND MANAGEMENT OPTIONS WILL BE DEVELOPED AND POSTED ON THE WWW.UVM.EDU/NWCROPS WEBSITE.SUBRECIPIENT:NO SUBAWARDS ARE INCLUDED IN THIS ASSISTANCE AGREEMENT.OUTCOMES:DELIVERABLES:

DESCRIPTION:THE OVERALL OBJECTIVE IS TO INCREASE KNOWLEDGE OF IPM PRACTICES IN WEST VIRGINIA GROWERS, PESTICIDE APPLICATORS AND HOMEOWNERS USING THE EXISTENT WVUES OUTREACH PROGRAMS. OUR
SPECIFIC OBJECTIVES ARE AS FOLLOWS: (1) PRESENT TOPICS ABOUT IPM PESTICIDE SAFETY TO FRUIT GROWERS, VEGETABLE GROWERS AND MASTER GARDENERS. 1. APPLICANT PROPOSES TO CONDUCT A TOTAL OF FOUR FRUIT MEETINGS PER YEAR IN THE HAMPSHIRE COUNTY AREA TO INCREASE KNOWLEDGE ABOUT PEST BIOLOGY AND CONTROL OF INSECT PEST.

DESCRIPTION:INSECT PESTS REDUCE PLANT HEALTH AND MARKETABILITY, WHICH FREQUENTLY REQUIRES CHEMICAL INTERVENTION TO PRODUCE A SALEABLE PRODUCT. POLLINATORS PLAY A CRITICAL ROLE IN U.S. AGRICULTURE AND ECOSYSTEM FUNCTIONING, BUT INSECTICIDES USED DURING CROP PRODUCTION CAN HARM THEM. MOST POLLINATOR TOXICOLOGICAL DATA FOCUS ON BEES. LEPIDOPTERAN POLLINATORS (I.E., BUTTERFLIES AND MOTHS) DIFFER IN SUSCEPTIBILITY TO INSECTICIDES COMPARED TO BEES AND HAVE DIFFERENT ROUTES OF EXPOSURE (E.G., LARVAL LEAF-FEEDING VERSUS NECTAR CONSUMPTION). CONSEQUENTLY, PRODUCTS COMPATIBLE WITH BEE CONSERVATION POSE AN UNKNOWN RISK TO LEPIDOPTERAN POLLINATORS, YET BOTH TAXA ARE PERVASIVE IN AGROECOSYSTEMS. FLORIDA'S ORNAMENTAL PLANT INDUSTRY HAS AN ESTIMATED ECONOMIC IMPACT OF $21 BILLION, RANKING SECOND IN THE NATION. ORNAMENTAL PLANTS ARE A MAJOR COMPONENT OF RAPIDLY EXPANDING URBAN LANDSCAPES WHERE 80% OF THE U.S. POPULATION RESIDES, THUS MAKING PESTICIDE USE DURING PRODUCTION AND PERENNIAL MAINTENANCE RELEVANT TO MOST PEOPLE AND TO FUTURE LANDSCAPES. WE WILL DEVELOP LEPIDOPTERA IPPM STRATEGIES FOR ORNAMENTAL PLANT PRODUCTION, USE OUR RESULTS TO CREATE A WILDLIFE-FRIENDLY PLANT PRODUCTION CERTIFICATION PROGRAM, AND INCREASE THE POSITIVE ENVIRONMENTAL AND ECOLOGICAL IMPACT OF ORNAMENTAL PLANT PRODUCTION.ACTIVITIES:1. REVIEW NOVEL DATA DOCUMENTING THE ACUTE, CHRONIC, LETHAL, AND SUBLETHAL EFFECTS OF PESTICIDES TO MONARCHS OVER TIME.
2. PUBLISH IN TWO SCIENTIFIC PUBLICATIONS DISCUSSING PROJECT RESULTS AND IMPLICATIONS SUBMITTED TO AN OPEN-SOURCE, PEER REVIEWED JOURNALS.
3. DISTILL AND REVIEW ANY NEW EVIDENCE-BASED IPPM RECOMMENDATIONS FOR CONTROLLING KEY SAP-FEEDING INSECT PESTS WHILE MITIGATING IMPACTS TO LEPIDOPTERAN POLLINATORS IN A PLANT PRODUCTION ENVIRONMENT.
4. DEVELOP A COST-BENEFIT ANALYSIS TO DETERMINE ECONOMIC VIABILITY AND EFFECTIVENESS OF IPPM RECOMMENDATIONS.
5. CONCEPTUALIZE AND PRODUCE FIVE WEB-BASED TRAINING MODULES TO CERTIFY INDUSTRY PROFESSIONALS IN THE USE OF IPPM STRATEGIES TO PROTECT INSECT POLLINATORS DURING ORNAMENTAL PLANT PRODUCTION.
6. PUBLISH IN TWO INDUSTRY TRADE JOURNAL ARTICLES AND TWO EXTENSION PUBLICATIONS DISSEMINATING OUR IPPM RECOMMENDATIONS.
7. PARTICIPATE IN ONE LOCAL, ONE STATEWIDE, ONE REGIONAL, AND ONE NATIONAL TRAINING EVENT TO INCREASE AWARENESS OF IPPM PRODUCTION PRACTICES.
8. DEVELOP FINAL REPORT SUMMARIZING ALL PROJECT ACTIVITIES, DATA, PUBLICATIONS, AND QUANTIFIED OUTCOMES.SUBRECIPIENT:THE APPLICANT HAS BUDGETED A SUBCONTRACT TO UF ICBR INSECTICIDE RESIDUE ANALYSIS. IN ADDITION TO QUANTIFYING EFFECTS OF INSECTICIDES ON MONARCHS VIA EXPOSURE THROUGH HERBIVORY,THE APPLICANT WILL QUANTIFY POTENTIAL EXPOSURE TO BUTTERFLIES AND OTHER POLLINATORS VIA THE MOVEMENT OF INSECTICIDES INTO MILKWEED NECTAR. TROPICAL MILKWEED FLOWERS ARE ACTIVELY VISITED BY BEES AND BUTTERFLIES, AND EVIDENCE SUGGESTS THAT SOME SYSTEMIC INSECTICIDES CAN REDUCE MONARCH ADULT LONGEVITY VIA NECTAR FEEDING AND THUS MAY SERVE AS ANOTHER ROUTE FOR NON-TARGET INSECTICIDE IMPACTS (JAMES 2019). WE WILL OBTAIN A. CURASSAVICA PLANTS (12 PER INSECTICIDE TREATMENT) THAT ARE IN FULL BLOOM, PRUNE OFF ALL FLOWERS, MAKE FOLIAR INSECTICIDE APPLICATIONS, AND MAINTAIN THE PLANTS USING STANDARD PRODUCTION PRACTICES TO PROMOTE NEW FLOWER DEVELOPMENT. DESCRIPTION:THE OVERALL GOAL OF THE PROPOSED WORK IS TO INCREASE GROWER ADOPTION OF INTEGRATED PEST AND POLLINATOR MANAGEMENT TACTICS IN WATERMELON PRODUCTION. THROUGH A STATEWIDE SCOUTING PROGRAM, THE GRANTEE WILL PARTNER WITH ILLINOIS AND INDIANA GROWERS TO ADDRESS THE CHALLENGES IMPLEMENTING IPPM PRACTICES ON THEIR FARMS. IN TOTAL, THE GRANTEE EXPECTS THIS PROJECT TO IMPACT OVER 200 WATERMELON GROWERS AND THE 6,000 ACRES OF FARMLAND THEY COLLECTIVELY MANAGE. FUNDS WILL BE USED TO SUPPORT A GRADUATE STUDENT TO CONDUCT ON-FARM DEMONSTRATION TRIALS DURING THE SUMMERS OF 2022 AND 2023. IN ACCOMPLISHING THESE GOALS, THE GRANTEE ANTICIPATE REDUCING INSECTICIDE/MITICIDE APPLICATIONS IN SEEDLESS WATERMELON PRODUCTION BY 30% (2-3 SPRAYS PER SEASON) COMPARED TO CURRENT REGIMES, WHICH WILL HAVE A NUMBER OF SHORT- AND LONG-TERM BENEFITS TO GROWERS. THE GRANTEE EXPECTS DECREASED INSECTICIDE APPLICATION, INCREASED POLLINATION, AND IMPROVED FRUIT QUALITY WITH INCREASED IPPM ADOPTION IN THE SHORT-TERM. IN THE LONG-TERM, THE GRANTEE ANTICIPATES GREATER ACCESS TO PRIME MARKETS (I.E., WAL-MART'S NEW POLICY MANDATING ADOPTION OF IPM AND 'BEE-SAFE PRACTICES' BY 2025) DUE TO DECREASED PESTICIDE RESIDUES AND INCREASED VIABILITY OF AGROECOSYSTEMS THROUGH A REDUCTION IN OFF-TARGET EFFECTS FROM INSECTICIDE USE.ACTIVITIES:1. GRANTEE WILL IMPLEMENT A THRESHOLD-BASED SCOUTING PROGRAM TO INCREASE GROWER ADOPTION OF INTEGRATED PEST AND POLLINATOR MANAGEMENT PRACTICES.
A: GRANTEE CREATED AN IPPM PROGRAM WITH THE PRIMARY GOAL OF INCREASING THESE PRACTICES IN SEEDLESS WATERMELON PRODUCTION. THIS WILL REQUIRE INTENSIVE COLLABORATIONS WITH GROWERS, IN WHICH GRANTEE WILL SCOUT GROWER FIELDS AND PROVIDE WEEKLY REPORTS AND RECOMMENDATIONS FOR PRIMARY ARTHROPOD PESTS. EACH PARTICIPATING GROWER WILL SELECT ONE 20-30 ACRE WATERMELON FIELD TO BE SCOUTED WEEKLY. PLANTS WILL BE INSPECTED FOR THE PRESENCE OF COMMON ARTHROPOD PESTS. MOST PESTS WILL BE SAMPLED ON A PER PLANT BASIS (E.G., STRIPED CUCUMBER BEETLES, APHIDS, SQUASH BUGS); HOWEVER, SOME WILL BE ON A PER FLOWER OR PER LEAF BASIS. MITES WILL BE COUNTED ON THREE LEAVES PER PLANT USING A LOUPE (~240 LEAVES PER FIELD). PICKLEWORM LARVAE WILL BE SAMPLED BY PULLING 4 MALE FLOWERS PER PLANT AND EXAMINING EACH FOR ENTRY HOLES (~320 FLOWERS PER FIELD). AT THE END OF EACH GROWING SEASON, GRANTEE WILL REQUEST THAT GROWERS SUBMIT THEIR PESTICIDE RECORDS OF FIELDS SAMPLED BY THE SCOUT (I.E., PRODUCTS, RATES, DATES OF APPLICATION). PESTICIDE APPLICATION RECORDS WILL BE COMPARED WITH WEEKLY SCOUTING DATA TO DETERMINE WHETHER THE GROWER COMPLIED WITH OUR IPPM GUIDELINES (I.E., FOLLOWING THE THRESHOLD AND/OR APPLYING PRODUCTS WITH REDUCED BEE TOXICITY VALUES). GRANTEE WILL MEET WITH ALL GROWERS ON AN ANNUAL BASIS TO DISCUSS INSECTICIDE RECORDS, IN ADDITION TO WHAT STRATEGIES EITHER WORKED OR DID NOT WORK TO MANAGE THE TARGET ARTHROPOD PESTS. GROWERS WILL ALSO BE REQUIRED TO SUBMIT A SURVEY IN WHICH THEY DESCRIBE THEIR EXPERIENCES EITHER FOLLOWING OR FORGOING THE IPPM RECOMMENDATIONS.

2. GRANTEE WILL EVALUATE THE IMPACT OF IPPM PRACTICES ON WATERMELON QUALITY (POLLINATION AND INSECTICIDE RESIDUES).
A: GRANTEE WILL LEVERAGE DATA COLLECTED FROM OBJECTIVE 1. GRANTEE WILL PAIR POLLINATION OBSERVATIONS, YIELD ESTIMATES, AND RESIDUE SAMPLES TO CONDUCT POST-HOC ANALYSES AIMED AT CORRELATING THE ADOPTION OF IPPM PRACTICES WITH WATERMELON MARKETABILITY OUTCOMES. GRANTEE WILL CONDUCT POLLINATION OBSERVATIONS IN ALL TREATMENT FIELDS DURING THE BLOOM PERIOD. ALL 10 FARMS WILL BE VISITED BIWEEKLY OVER A FOUR-WEEK PERIOD (2 OBSERVATIONS PER FARM). GRANTEE WILL CONDUCT YIELD ASSESSMENTS AT TWO POINTS DURING THE GROWING SEASON, DURING BLOOM AND ONE WEEK BEFORE HARVEST. THE SIZE OF ALL MELONS WILL BE MEASURED (LENGTH AND WIDTH OF MELONS) USING A MEASURING TAPE WITHIN SAMPLING AREAS, 2M TIMES; 2M. THIS WILL BE REPLICATED 40 TIMES IN EACH FIELD. GRANTEE WILL HARVEST 20 MELONS FROM EACH FIELD TO EXTRACT INSECTICIDE RESIDUES

Recipient: MINNESOTA CHIPPEWA TRIBE - WHITE EARTH BAND
Description:
DESCRIPTION:THE WHITE EARTH PESTICIDES PROGRAM IS IMPLEMENTING A COMPREHENSIVE QUALITY MANAGEMENT PROGRAM PLAN (QMPRP) TO MANAGE THE COLLECTION AND ANALYSIS OF PESTICIDE-RELATED SAMPLES. THIS PROGRAM IS IN COLLABORATION WITH THE MINNESOTA DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE (MDA) LABORATORY. THE MDA LAB HAS ENDORSED WHITE EARTH'S QMPRP AND IS RESPONSIBLE FOR CONDUCTING ANALYSES ON BEHALF OF THE TRIBE TO ASSESS PESTICIDE RESIDUES IN VARIOUS ENVIRONMENTAL MEDIA.

TWO PRIMARY ANALYTICAL METHODS ARE EMPLOYED IN THIS PROJECT: GAS CHROMATOGRAPHY/MASS SPECTROMETRY (GC/MS) AND LIQUID CHROMATOGRAPHY/MASS SPECTROMETRY (LC/MS/MS). THE GC/MS/MS METHOD TARGETS 38 DIFFERENT COMPOUNDS, WHILE THE LC/MS/MS METHOD COVERS AN EXTENSIVE LIST OF 137 ANALYTES, INCLUDING VARIOUS FUNGICIDES, HERBICIDES, RODENTICIDES, AND INSECTICIDES COMMONLY USED IN PINE POINT AND OTHER AGRICULTURAL AREAS WITHIN THE RESERVATION BOUNDARIES.

SAMPLE COLLECTION ADHERES TO STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURES (SOP) OUTLINED IN THE QMPRP. SPECIFICALLY, WILD RICE AND WATER SAMPLES ARE OBTAINED FROM THE SAME LOCATIONS AT EACH SAMPLING SITE. DETAILED SURFACE WATER FIELD DATA ARE COLLECTED AND DOCUMENTED, ENCOMPASSING INFORMATION SUCH AS DATE, TIME, WATER TEMPERATURE, DISSOLVED OXYGEN LEVELS, PH, TURBIDITY, CONDUCTIVITY, WEATHER CONDITIONS, AIR TEMPERATURE, WIND SPEED, DEW POINT, AND HUMIDITY.

BEFORE COLLECTING WILD RICE SAMPLES, THE DENSITY OF THE WILD RICE BED AT THE SAMPLING SITE IS ASSESSED USING A ONE-METER QUADRAT AND RECORDED. WILD RICE SAMPLES ARE OBTAINED BY CAREFULLY UPROOTING THE ENTIRE PLANT, INCLUDING ITS ROOTS. MEASUREMENTS ARE TAKEN FOR THE LENGTH OF THE STALK AND ROOTS, AND THESE VALUES ARE DOCUMENTED. IN SOME CASES, THE ROOTS AND STALKS OF WILD RICE MAY BE ANALYZED SEPARATELY TO INVESTIGATE POTENTIAL DIFFERENCES IN THE STORAGE AND UPTAKE OF VARIOUS ANALYTES.

EACH SAMPLING SITE YIELDS THREE SURFACE WATER SAMPLES AND THREE WILD RICE SAMPLES, WHERE WILD RICE IS PRESENT. THESE SAMPLES ARE DESIGNATED FOR DIFFERENT PURPOSES, INCLUDING GC ANALYSIS, LC ANALYSIS, AND SPECIFIC TESTING FOR GLYPHOSATE, GLUFOSINATE, AND AMPA COMPOUNDS. THIS PROJECT AIMS TO PROVIDE VALUABLE INSIGHTS INTO PESTICIDE RESIDUES IN THE ENVIRONMENT AND THEIR POTENTIAL IMPACT ON THE WHITE EARTH TRIBE'S AGRICULTURAL REGIONS.ACTIVITIES:THE PROJECT INVOLVES THE FOLLOWING KEY ACTIVITIES: 
 
1.	IMPLEMENTATION OF A QUALITY MANAGEMENT PROGRAM PLAN (QMPRP) FOR COLLECTING PESTICIDE-RELATED SAMPLES.
2.	COLLABORATION WITH THE MINNESOTA DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE (MDA) LABORATORY FOR SAMPLE ANALYSIS.
3.	UTILIZATION OF GAS CHROMATOGRAPHY/MASS SPECTROMETRY (GC/MS) AND LIQUID CHROMATOGRAPHY/MASS SPECTROMETRY (LC/MS/MS) METHODS FOR PESTICIDE RESIDUE ANALYSIS.
4.	COLLECTION OF WILD RICE AND WATER SAMPLES AT DESIGNATED SAMPLING SITES.
5.	DOCUMENTATION OF SURFACE WATER FIELD DATA, INCLUDING VARIOUS ENVIRONMENTAL PARAMETERS.
6.	MEASUREMENT AND DOCUMENTATION OF WILD RICE BED DENSITY, STALK LENGTH, AND ROOT LENGTH.
7.	SEPARATION OF WILD RICE ROOTS AND STALKS FOR POTENTIAL SEPARATE ANALYSIS.
8.	PROPER HANDLING OF WILD RICE SAMPLES ACCORDING TO ESTABLISHED STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURES (SOP).
9.	COLLECTION OF THREE SURFACE WATER SAMPLES AND THREE WILD RICE SAMPLES AT EACH SITE FOR SPECIFIC ANALYSES, INCLUDING GC, LC, AND TESTING FOR GLYPHOSATE, GLUFOSINATE, AND AMPA COMPOUNDS.

SUBRECIPIENT:NO SUBAWARDS ARE INCLUDED IN THIS ASSISTANCE AGREEMENT.OUTCOMES:PROJECT OUTCOMES:

1)	IDENTIFICATION OF PESTICIDES OF INTEREST AND PESTICIDES OF CONCERN: THE ANALYSIS WILL YIELD INFORMATION TO IDENTIFY SPECIFIC PESTICIDES THAT ARE OF INTEREST DUE TO THEIR PRESENCE AND POTENTIAL ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT. IT WILL ALSO HIGHLIGHT PESTICIDES OF CONCERN, WHICH MAY HAVE ADVERSE EFFECTS ON THE ENVIRONMENT.
2)	INFORMED SAMPLING AND MONITORING PLANS: THE PROJECT OUTCOMES WILL GUIDE THE DEVELOPMENT OF FUTURE SAMPLING AND MONITORING PLANS, ENSURING THAT RESOURCES ARE ALLOCATED EFFECTIVELY TO AREAS WITH IDENTIFIED PESTICIDE CONCERNS.

DESCRIPTION:THE AGREEMENT PROVIDES FUNDING TO THE EXTENSION FOUNDATION. SPECIFICALLY, THE RECIPIENT WILL ADMINISTER A NATIONAL SUBAWARD PROGRAM THAT ENABLES OR INCREASES THE CAPACITY OF PESTICIDE SAFETY EDUCATION PROGRAMS (PSEPS) TO DEVELOP PROGRAMS AND MATERIALS FOR PERSONS PREPARING TO BECOME PESTICIDE APPLICATORS OR MAINTAIN A CERTIFICATION. THE PURPOSE OF THIS COOPERATIVE AGREEMENT IS TO ENSURE THAT RESTRICTED USE PESTICIDES ARE USED SAFELY AND DO NOT CAUSE UNREASONABLE ADVERSE EFFECTS TO PERSONS WHO USE THEM, THE PUBLIC, OR THE ENVIRONMENT.ACTIVITIES:THE ACTIVITIES INCLUDE 1) ADMINISTERING AND CONTINUING TO IMPROVE THE NATIONAL SUBAWARD PROGRAM (APPLICATION, SUBAWARD DISBURSEMENT, REPORTING, MONITORING AND EVALUATION); 2) ENCOURAGING, FACILITATING, AND INCENTIVIZING SUBAWARD COLLABORATIONS BETWEEN LAND GRANT UNIVERSITIES AND MINORITY-SERVING INSTITUTIONS (MSIS), INCLUDING THROUGH THE SHARING AND ADAPTATION OF PESTICIDE SAFETY EDUCATION MATERIALS; 3) PENDING ANNUAL FUNDING AWARD AND AGENCY BUDGET, CONDUCTING ADDITIONAL PESTICIDE SAFETY EDUCATION ENVIRONMENTAL JUSTICE ACTIVITIES, SUCH AS AT MSIS.SUBRECIPIENT:SUBAWARDS ARE TO SO-CALLED 'PESTICIDE SAFETY EDUCATION PROGRAMS' (PSEPS) OR 'PESTICIDE SAFETY EDUCATION FUNDS MANAGEMENT PROGRAMS' (PSEFMP PROGRAMS), USUALLY LOCATED AT LAND GRANT UNIVERSITIES. SUBAWARD RECIPIENTS WILL DEVELOP OR UPDATE MATERIALS, CONDUCT OUTREACH PROGRAMS, PROVIDE TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE, AND/OR OFFER EDUCATION AND TRAINING ACTIVITIES FOR PERSONS PREPARING TO BECOME PESTICIDE APPLICATORS OR MAINTAIN A PESTICIDE APPLICATOR CERTIFICATION. PENDING ANNUAL BUDGET AND AGENCY FUNDING, SUBAWARD RECIPIENTS MAY ALSO IMPLEMENT ADDITIONAL ENVIRONMENTAL JUSTICE ACTIVITIES RELATED TO PESTICIDE SAFETY EDUCATION.OUTCOMES:THE ANTICIPATED DELIVERABLES INCLUDE 1) A USER-FRIENDLY ONLINE APPLICATION PROCESS; 2) EFFICIENT SUBRECIPIENT AWARD DISTRIBUTION; 3) AN ONLINE SUPPORT NETWORK FOR SUBRECIPIENTS; 4) A USER-FRIENDLY REPORTING SYSTEM; 5) TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE (MATERIALS, WORKSHOPS, AND MEETINGS) TO SUBRECIPIENTS; 6) EDUCATIONAL MATERIALS THAT ARE CREATED, REVIEWED, AND SHARED WITHIN THE NETWORK; 7) PROGRAM MONITORING AND EVALUATION, INCLUDING THROUGH THE ESTABLISHMENT OF AN ADVISORY COMMITTEE; 8) QUARTERLY PROGRAMMATIC REPORTS; 9) BIANNUAL FINANCIAL REPORTS, AND AN END-OF-PROJECT FINANCIAL REPORT.

THE EXPECTED OUTCOMES INCLUDE 1) INCREASED PARTICIPATION FROM MSIS AND THE NATIONWIDE LAND GRANT UNIVERSITY SYSTEM THROUGH THE STREAMLINED APPLICATION PROCESS; 2) IMPROVED CAPACITY AT EXF TO ADMINISTER THE AWARDS; 3) COLLABORATIONS BETWEEN SUBAWARD RECIPIENTS ON, AND THE SHARING AND ADAPTATION OF, PESTICIDE SAFETY EDUCATION MATERIALS FOR PESTICIDE APPLICATORS; 4) AN IMPROVED AWARD STRUCTURE AT THE END OF THE FIVE YEARS; 5) IMPROVED CAPACITY AT MSIS TO ENGAGE IN PESTICIDE SAFETY EDUCATION AND OUTREACH; AND 6) AVAILABILITY OF CULTURALLY RELEVANT EDUCATIONAL MATERIALS AND TRAINING OPPORTUNITIES TO UNDERSERVED AND MARGINALIZED POPULATIONS.

THE INTENDED BENEFICIARIES INCLUDE PEOPLE PREPARING TO BECOME PESTICIDE APPLICATORS OR MAINTAIN A CERTIFICATION.

DESCRIPTION:THE OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY (OSU) IS CONDUCTING OUTREACH TO SPREAD AWARENESS ABOUT THE SPOTTED LANTERNFLY (SLF). THE OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY EXTENSION AND OTHER DEPARTMENTS HAVE SEVERAL ONGOING SLF OUTREACH PROJECTS CURRENTLY ACTIVE STATEWIDE. SLF IS A PEST THAT HAS EQUAL CHANCES OF BEING FOUND IN RURAL AREAS NEAR CULTIVATED AGRICULTURAL CROPS OR IN DENSE CITYSCAPES NEAR MAJOR TRANSPORTATION CORRIDORS. ACTIVITIES:PROJECT ACTIVITIES INCLUDE 3D PRINTING THE SLF EGG MASSES AND OTHER LIFE STAGES FOR EDUCATION AND TRAINING PURPOSES AND WILL BE DISTRIBUTED TO OSU'S PARTNER AGENCIES. TO REACH THE GENERAL PUBLIC IN URBAN AREAS, OSU WILL RECRUIT FOOD AND ICE CREAM TRUCK VENDORS WHO ARE INTERESTED TO DISPLAY THE SLF MESSAGE VIA MAGNETIC SIGNS WITH QR CODES ON THEIR VEHICLES. OSU WILL ALSO ORGANIZE A TRI-STATE MEETING WHERE INFORMATION AND RESOURCES WILL BE SHARED TO PREPARE GROWERS, INDUSTRY AND THE GENERAL PUBLIC FOR AN INCREASE IN THE SLF POPULATION IN THE REGION.SUBRECIPIENT:NO SUBAWARDS ARE INCLUDED IN THIS ASSISTANCE AGREEMENT.OUTCOMES:THE INTENDED BENEFICIARIES ARE GROWERS, INDUSTRY, AND THE PUBLIC.

DESCRIPTION:UNDER THIS PROJECT, THE OHIO DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE (ODA) WILL SPREAD AWARENESS AMONG OHIO RESIDENTS ABOUT AN INVASIVE, DESTRUCTIVE PLANTHOPPER KNOWN AS THE SPOTTED LANTERNFLY (SLF). THE SPOTTED LANTERNFLY (LYCORMA DELICATULA) IS CURRENTLY IDENTIFIED IN FOUR OHIO COUNTIES (HAMILTON, JEFFERSON, LORAIN, AND CUYAHOGA); IT HAS BEEN DESIGNATED A DESTRUCTIVE PLANT PEST, AND IS A THREAT TO GRAPEVINES, TREE FRUIT, NURSERY STOCK AND TIMBER. THE KEY TO CONTROLLING SLF IS THE PROMPT IDENTIFICATION AND REPORTING OF SLF POPULATIONS, SO THAT THEY CAN BE ERADICATED BEFORE SPREADING FURTHER. ONE AREA OF OUTREACH UNDER THIS PROJECT TARGETS UNDERSERVED PUBLIC SCHOOLS IN LARGER CITIES. MANY GRADES HAVE WITHIN THEIR SCIENCE CURRICULUM A SECTION ON INVASIVE SPECIES. PROVIDING SLF MODEL KITS TO URBAN TEACHERS FOR USE IN THEIR CLASSROOMS, WILL GIVE TEACHERS A FUN PROJECT THAT FITS THE CURRICULUM. IT ALSO PROVIDES AN OPPORTUNITY FOR STUDENTS TO LEARN ABOUT SPOTTED LANTERNFLY AND TO BRING HOME A VISUAL REMINDER OF THE PEST TO LOOK OUT FOR. ACTIVITIES:THE OHIO DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE (ODA) WILL PARTNER WITH PUBLIC SCHOOL SCIENCE TEACHERS IN THE CITY OF CINCINNATI AND CITY OF CLEVELAND SCHOOL DISTRICTS WHERE SLF IS CURRENTLY FOUND. SCIENCE CURRICULA IN MANY GRADES HAVE UNITS ON INVASIVE SPECIES. ODA WILL CONTACT CURRICULUM SPECIALISTS IN EACH DISTRICT. THIS WILL SERVE THE DUAL PURPOSE OF IDENTIFYING THE GRADE LEVELS AT WHICH THE CLASSROOM PUZZLE KITS WILL BEST FIT THE CURRICULA AND HAVING A POINT OF CONTACT FOR DISTRIBUTION OF 75 KITS TO 75 CLASSROOMS.  ODA WILL SECURE SLF CLASSROOM KITS THROUGH IDEA COLLECTIVE, LLC IN NEW YORK, AND DISTRIBUTE THE KITS TO INTERESTED TEACHERS THROUGH THE CURRICULUM SPECIALISTS.SUBRECIPIENT:NO SUBAWARDS ARE INCLUDED IN THIS ASSISTANCE AGREEMENT.OUTCOMES:AS THE OHIO DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE (ODA) RECEIVES REPORTS OF SLF SIGHTINGS, THEY WILL USE QR CODES TO IDENTIFY WHERE THE REPORTS ORIGINATE AND WILL ASK THOSE WHO REPORT TO INDICATE WHERE THEY LEARNED ABOUT SLF.  THIS PROJECT WILL BE SUCCESSFUL IF THE NUMBER OF SLF SIGHTINGS INCREASES OVER THE PROJECT PERIOD (AND AFTERWARD), AND THE GEOGRAPHIC RANGE OF THE SIGHTINGS DOESN'T EXPAND (OR PERHAPS SHRINKS).

IN ADDITION TO THE CLASSROOM KITS, ZOO GRAPHICS, AND SOUVENIR CUPS DISTRIBUTED TO PARTICIPANTS FOR AWARENESS, THE ODA WILL PREPARE A MID-PROJECT WRITTEN REPORT FOR THE MONTHS OF MAY THROUGH OCTOBER 2023 AND A FINAL WRITTEN REPORT FOR THE MONTHS OF OCTOBER 2023 THROUGH APRIL 2024. THE REPORTS SHALL INCLUDE METRICS AND DESCRIPTIONS OF ACCOMPLISHMENTS UNDER EACH OBJECTIVE, INCLUDING ANY HIGHLIGHTS OR LESSONS LEARNED, CHALLENGES ENCOUNTERED, AND THE STEPS TAKEN TO RESOLVE THEM, AND ANY OTHER RELEVANT INFORMATION ABOUT THE PROJECT. 

THE INTENDED BENEFICIARIES OF THIS PROJECT ARE THE RESIDENTS OF THE STATE OF OHIO.

DESCRIPTION:THE PURPOSE OF THIS AWARD IS TO PROVIDE FINANCIAL AND TECHNICAL SUPPORT CHEROKEE NATION AND THE INTER-TRIBAL ENVIRONMENTAL COUNCIL (ITEC) IN THEIR IMPLEMENTATION OF PESTICIDE PROGRAMS. THIS COOPERATIVE AGREEMENT CONTINUES THE RECIPIENT'S IMPLEMENTATION OF ITS PESTICIDE PROGRAMS. THE PRIMARY GOAL OF THE PESTICIDES PROGRAM IS TO IMPROVE HUMAN HEALTH AND THE ENVIRONMENT AMONG REGION 6 TRIBES BY BUILDING TRIBAL CAPACITY TO ESTABLISH, IMPLEMENT, AND MAINTAIN PESTICIDES PROGRAMS. THIS CAPACITY WILL BE BUILT WITH FIVE MAIN PROGRAMMATIC OBJECTIVES: STAFF TRAINING AND CAPACITY BUILDING, REPORTING PROGRAM PROGRESS, PESTICIDES TECHNICAL MANAGEMENT ASSISTANCE, TRIBAL COORDINATION AND OUTREACH, AND THE PESTICIDE APPLICATION DATABASE OUTREACH.
ACTIVITIES:THE PESTICIDE PROGRAM WILL IMPLEMENT A PROACTIVE APPROACH TO: (1) PROVIDE TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO EACH TRIBE; (2) ASSIST, THROUGH TRAINING, TO EACH TRIBAL PERSONNEL AND MANAGERS; (3) ENGAGE, THROUGH OUTREACH AND EDUCATION, WITH MEMBER TRIBES IN ALL ASPECTS OF THEIR PESTICIDES MANAGEMENT; (4) PREPARE AND PROVIDE REPORTS TO EPA REGION 6 ON A QUARTERLY BASIS FOR ACTIVITIES ASSOCIATED WITH TRIBAL PESTICIDE PROGRAM AND (5) INCREASE PROGRAM DEVELOPMENT AND CAPACITY BY OBTAINING THE NECESSARY TECHNICAL AND PROGRAMMATIC EXPERTISE REQUIRED TO ADMINISTER A PESTICIDES PROGRAM.
SUBRECIPIENT:NO SUBAWARDS ARE INCLUDED IN THIS ASSISTANCE AGREEMENT.OUTCOMES:THIS AGREEMENT WILL RESULT IN ENHANCED PROTECTION OF HUMAN HEALTH AND THE ENVIRONMENT (E.G.) REDUCED PESTICIDE EXPOSURE TO AGRICULTURAL WORKERS AND HANDLERS, PROTECTION OF WATER RESOURCES FROM PESTICIDES, AND PROTECTION OF ENDANGERED SPECIES. DELIVERABLES INCLUDE QUARTERLY PERFORMANCE REPORTS, OUTREACH MATERIALS DEVELOPED FOR REGION 6 TRIBES SUCH AS PAMPHLETS OR BROCHURES, THE ANNUAL ITEC CONFERENCE AND THE TRIBAL ENVIRONMENTAL SUMMIT.

DESCRIPTION:THE PURPOSE OF THIS PROPOSAL IS TO STRENGTHEN SUSTAINABLE PEST MANAGEMENT IN THE PACIFIC NORTHWEST (PNW) BY SLOWING THE DEVELOPMENT OF HERBICIDE RESISTANT WEEDS, PRESERVING THE USEFUL LIFE OF CURRENT HERBICIDE PRODUCTS, AND IMPROVING ADOPTION OF EXISTING INTEGRATED PEST MANAGEMENT (IPM) TOOLS. WE WILL ACCOMPLISH THESE GOALS BY TRAINING AGRICULTURAL PROFESSIONALS ON HERBICIDE RESISTANCE PLANNING, MANAGEMENT, AND REMEDIATION, THUS EMPOWERING THESE PROFESSIONALS TO TRANSFER AND AMPLIFY THE SKILLS AND KNOWLEDGE GAINED IN OUR WORKSHOPS TO GROWERS. THIS PROJECT WILL BE BROADLY APPLICABLE TO THE DIVERSITY OF CONVENTIONALLY-PRODUCED FIELD CROP OPERATIONS ON 9.7 MILLION ACRES OF IRRIGATED AND NON-IRRIGATED LAND IN THE PNW, WILL SERVE AS A TEMPLATE FOR PROGRAMS IN OTHER REGIONS OF THE UNITED STATES, AND FOR FUTURE IPM RESISTANCE PROGRAMS TARGETING INSECTS AND DISEASES IN THE PNW.ACTIVITIES:1. YEAR 1: APPLICANT WILL DEVELOP CURRICULA FOR UP TO FOUR ONE-HOUR, WEB-BASED, ON-DEMAND TRAINING MODULES BROADLY APPLICABLE TO THE DIVERSITY OF CONVENTIONALLY-PRODUCED FIELD CROP OPERATIONS ON 9.7 MILLION ACRES OF IRRIGATED AND NON-IRRIGATED LAND IN THE PACIFIC NORTH WEST. ONLINE, INTERACTIVE TRAINING MODULES WILL BE AVAILABLE IN ENGLISH AND SPANISH. APPLICANT WILL DEVELOP TRAINING MATERIALS ON HERBICIDE RESISTANCE MANAGEMENT PLANS, BEST MANAGEMENT PRACTICES, AND REMEDIAL ACTION PLANTS. INFORMATION WILL BE INCLUDED IN ONLINE TRAINING MODULES.
2. YEAR 2: APPLICANT WILL HOST THREE REGION-SPECIFIC, IN-PERSON WORKSHOPS FOR STAKEHOLDERS REPRESENTING OVER 1.58 MILLION ACRES OF AGRICULTURAL LAND IN OREGON'S WILLAMETTE VALLEY, 8 MILLION ACRES OF AGRICULTURAL LAND IN THE COLUMBIA BASIN OF OREGON AND WASHINGTON, AND 387,000 ACRES OF UNDER-REPRESENTED AGRICULTURAL LAND IN THE TREASURE VALLEY OF OREGON AND IDAHO. SUBRECIPIENT:NO SUBAWARDS ARE INCLUDED IN THIS ASSISTANCE AGREEMENT.OUTCOMES:ACTIVITES:
1. UP TO FOUR ONE-HOUR WEB-BASED TRAINING MODULES ON HERBICIDE RESISTANCE IN PNW SYSTEMS, HOSTED BY OSU'S PACE PLATFORM.
2.THREE REGION-SPECIFIC, IN-PERSON WORKSHOPS ON HERBICIDE RESISTANCE DELIVERED AT OSU'S RESEARCH AND EXPERIMENTS CENTERS TO COVER CROPPING SYSTEMS IN THE WILLAMETTE VALLEY, THE COLUMBIA BASIN OF OREGON AND WASHINGTON, AND THE TREASURE VALLEY OF OREGON AND IDAHO.
3. AN EASILY-ADAPTABLE, TIME-BASED TEACHING PLAN PROVIDING LEARNING OBJECTIVES, LESSON PLANS, ACTIVITIES AND EVALUATION PLANS TO ASSIST WORKSHOP PARTICIPANTS IN TRANSFERRING HERBICIDE
RESISTANCE BMP'S AND PLANNING STRATEGIES.

BENIFICIARIES: PACIFIC NORTHWEST GROWERS, FARM WORKERS AGRICULTURAL PROFESSIONALS.

SHORT-TERM OUTCOMES:
PARTICIPANTS WILL BE ABLE TO 1) IDENTIFY KEY WEED SPECIES THAT HAVE SHOWN RESISTANCE TO HERBICIDES IN THEIR REGIONS, 2) EXPLAIN THE MECHANISMS THAT CONTRIBUTE TO HERBICIDE RESISTANCE BY WEEDS IN AGRICULTURAL SYSTEMS, 3) FIND AND USE AVAILABLE RESOURCES TO SELECT HERBICIDES BASED ON MODES-OF-ACTION FOR REGIONAL RESISTANT WEED SPECIES, 4) RECOGNIZE NON-CHEMICAL WEED MANAGEMENT TACTICS AS PART OF A COMPLETE IPM PROGRAM, 5) UNDERSTAND THE ENVIRONMENTAL AND ECONOMIC RISKS OF HERBICIDE RESISTANCE.

PARTICIPANTS WILL BE ABLE TO 1) MAKE INFORMED DECISIONS ABOUT WEED MANAGEMENT IN THEIR SYSTEMS, 2) IMPLEMENT WEED MANAGEMENT TECHNIQUES TO PREVENT RESISTANCE FROM OCCURRING, 3) INCORPORATE THESE SKILLS INTO AN IPM PLAN FOR WEEDS IN THEIR SYSTEM 4) TRANSFER KNOWLEDGE OF HERBICIDE RESISTANCE PLANNING STRATEGIES AND BMP'S TO GROWERS AND
FARM WORKERS ACROSS PNW, 5) OFFER EFFECTIVE TRAININGS ON HERBICIDE RESISTANCE ADAPTED TO THEIR REGION OR AUDIENCE, 6) ENCOURAGE SUSTAINABLE PEST MANAGEMENT IN PACIFIC NORTHWEST

DESCRIPTION:UNDER THIS AWARD OREGON STATE UNIVERSITY (OSU) WILL DISTRIBUTE COPIES OF EXPLORE THE BEES OF OREGON ACROSS THE PACIFIC NORTHWEST PORTION OF REGION 10 (EXCLUDING ALASKA) THROUGH THE OREGON STATE UNIVERSITY BEE ADVOCATE PROGRAM. THE ACTIVITY BOOK TARGETS AGES 4 AND ABOVE AND HIGHLIGHTS THE CONNECTION BETWEEN CROPS AND THE BEES OF THE STATE. THE BOOK ALSO DRAWS THE CONNECTION BETWEEN FIRST FOODS AND THE BEES THAT POLLINATE THEM AND WAS DEVELOPED IN PARTNERSHIP WITH THE OREGON STATE UNIVERSITY INDIGENOUS PEOPLE'S WORK GROUP. STUDENTS ENGAGED BY THE ACTIVITY BOOK WILL: (A) RECOGNIZE THE CONNECTION OF BEES TO CROPS IN THE REGION AND UNDERSTAND WHAT IS BEING DONE TO KEEP BEES FROM PESTICIDE EXPOSURE AND (B) BE LESS AFRAID OF STINGING INSECTS AND WILL BE LESS LIKELY TO USE PESTICIDES WHEN ENCOUNTERING BENEFICIAL INSECTS IN THEIR ADULTHOOD.

DESCRIPTION:THIS ACTION PROVIDES FUNDING IN THE AMOUNT OF $199,999 TO CLEMSON UNIVERSITY, SOUTH CAROLINA FOR THEIR CONTINUING PESTICIDE ENVIRONMENTAL PROGRAMS WHILE GIVING IT GREATER FLEXIBILITY TO ADDRESS ITS HIGHEST ENVIRONMENTAL PRIORITIES, IMPROVE ENVIRONMENTAL PERFORMANCE, ACHIEVE ADMINISTRATIVE SAVINGS AND STRENGTHEN THE PARTNERSHIP BETWEEN THE CLEMSON UNIVERSITY AND ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION AGENCY (EPA).ACTIVITIES:THIS AGREEMENT FUNDS STATEWIDE PROGRAMS TO: PROVIDE A COMPREHENSIVE PROGRAM OF REGISTRATION, LABELING, TRAINING, CERTIFICATION, INSPECTIONS AND ENFORCEMENT THAT WILL PREVENT UNNECESSARY EXPOSURE TO HUMANS, THE LAND AND WATER BY ENSURING THAT PESTICIDES ARE USED AND DISPOSED OF IN THE MANNER SPECIFIED ON EACH PRODUCT. DESCRIPTION:THE GOAL OF THIS PROJECT IS TO IMPROVE POLLINATOR PROTECTION AND HEALTH THROUGH TARGETED SURVEYS, TRAININGS, AND EVALUATIONS OF 5,000 NEWLY-TRAINED AND 2,500 PREVIOUSLY-TRAINED PESTICIDE APPLICATORS ACROSS AT LEAST 12 STATES TO ENSURE KNOWLEDGE TRANSFER IS SUCCESSFUL AND IMPACTFUL IN AFFECTING BEHAVIORAL PRACTICES REGARDING PESTICIDE USE AROUND POLLINATING INSECTS THAT ALIGN WITH AIMS OF MANAGED POLLINATOR PROTECTION PLANS. 
ACTIVITIES:#9679; OBJECTIVE 1: ASSESS EDUCATIONAL NEEDS AND TRAINING FORMATS FOR LICENSED U.S. PESTICIDE APPLICATORS.
O OUTPUTS: SURVEY OF ALL SLAS, TRIBES AND PSEP PROGRAMS IN THE US AND ANALYSIS OF TRAINING TO SEE THE EXTENT IT LINES UP WITH RECOMMENDATION FROM EPA ON LABEL AND MITIGATION AND WITH SLA AND TRIBAL MP3.
O OUTCOMES: STATE-SPECIFIC DATA ON PESTICIDE TRAINING SYSTEMS AND REQUIREMENTS THAT WILL AID IN THE DEVELOPMENT OF EFFECTIVE DELIVERY OF OUR TRAINING AS WELL AS OTHERS THAT AIM FOR A NATIONAL OR MULTI-STATE APPROACH
#9679; OBJECTIVE 2: SURVEY PREVIOUSLY TRAINED APPLICATORS AND DEVELOP EVALUATION TOOLS FOR TRAININGS ON LABEL COMPREHENSION AND IMPLEMENTATION OF POLLINATOR PROTECTION PRACTICES.
O OUTPUTS: DATA ON KNOWLEDGE GAINED BY PREVIOUSLY TRAINED APPLICATORS, ALLOWING FOR THE ASSESSMENT OF RETENTION AND BEHAVIORAL CHANGES OVER TIME. EVALUATION TOOL THAT CAN BE INCORPORATED INTO TRAINING DEVELOPED BY MP3 WORKING GROUP ON LABEL COMPREHENSION.
O OUTCOMES: TIME SERIES DATA ON RETENTION OF KNOWLEDGE, ADOPTION OF PRACTICES, AND IMPACT OF RE-TRAINING BASED ON DATA FROM OREGON'S 5+ YEARS OF POLLINATOR LABEL TRAININGS; THIS WILL HELP US UNDERSTAND TOPICS NEEDING MORE EMPHASIS AND TIME PERIODS THAT ARE VALUABLE FOR REINFORCING CONCEPTS. ACCURATE DATA ON POLLINATOR PROTECTION PLANS AND ACTIVITIES CAPTURED AS A RESULT OF EVALUATION TOOLS.
#9679; OBJECTIVE 3: ROLL OUT THE MP3 WORKING GROUP'S PESTICIDE LABEL TRAINING MODULE WITH NEW EVALUATION TOOLS TO AT LEAST 12 STATES.
O OUTPUTS: TRAININGS FOR PSEP STAFF AND EXTENSION ON HOW TO DELIVER THE TRAINING TO APPLICATORS, ANALYSIS OF EVALUATION TOOLS ON WHETHER THE TRAINING WAS EFFECTIVE AT IMPROVING KNOWLEDGE AND INCREASING INTENTION TO ADOPT NEW MITIGATION MEASURES TO PROTECT POLLINATORS.
O OUTCOMES: CONSISTENCY, ACCURACY, AND USABILITY IN THE AVAILABLE TRAINING MATERIALS AND NATIONAL OBJECTIVES THAT ALIGN WITH THE MP3 PESTICIDE LABEL TRAINING MODULE.

1. SURVEY OF ALL SLAS, TRIBES AND PSEP PROGRAMS IN THE US AND ANALYSIS OF TRAINING TO SEE THE EXTENT IT LINES UP WITH RECOMMENDATION FROM EPA ON LABEL AND MITIGATION AND WITH SLA AND TRIBAL MP3.
2. DATA ON KNOWLEDGE GAINED BY PREVIOUSLY TRAINED APPLICATORS, ALLOWING FOR THE ASSESSMENT OF RETENTION AND BEHAVIORAL CHANGES OVER TIME. EVALUATION TOOL THAT CAN BE INCORPORATED INTO TRAINING DEVELOPED BY MP3 WORKING GROUP ON LABEL COMPREHENSION.

DESCRIPTION:THE GOAL OF THIS PROJECT IS TO REDUCE NEONICOTINOID SEED TREATMENTS (NSTS) APPLIED TO ROW CROPS IN VERMONT. THE OBJECTIVE IS TO PROVIDE FARMERS EDUCATION AND EXPERIENCE WITH SCOUTING AND ASSESSING ACTION THRESHOLDS FOR EARLY SEASON PESTS IN ROW CROPS. THROUGH THIS NEWLY GAINED KNOWLEDGE FARMERS WILL BE ABLE TO MAKE MORE INFORMED DECISIONS AS TO WHETHER A NST IS NEEDED TO PREVENT ANY CROP LOSSES. THE OUTPUTS OF THE PROJECT INCLUDE SIX ON-FARM DEMONSTRATIONS, 30 ONE-ON-ONE CONSULTATIONS, SIX ON-FARM FIELDS DAYS, ONE WEBINAR SERIES, TWO CROP SPECIFIC SCOUTING FACTSHEETS, AND EIGHT PRESENTATIONS TO GROWER AND STAKEHOLDER AUDIENCES. THE APPLICANT EXPECTS TO EDUCATE OVER 1000 NORTHEASTERN GROWERS ON IPM PRACTICES, PROVIDE 250 FARMERS WITH HANDS-ON TRAINING, AND REDUCE NSTS ON AT LEAST 1500 ACRES.ACTIVITIES:A VARIETY OF ACTIVITIES WILL OCCUR TO EDUCATE FARMERS ON HOW TO SCOUT FOR SOIL PESTS, EVALUATE THRESHOLDS, AND DETERMINE PEST MANAGEMENT ACTIONS IN CORN AND SOYBEAN FIELDS IN VERMONT. ULTIMATELY, HELPING FARMERS IMPLEMENT INTEGRATED PEST MANAGEMENT (IPM) PRACTICES TO AVOID SUPERFLUOUS NSTS IF THEY ARE NOT NECESSARY.

1. SIX PARTNER FARMS WILL SERVE AS DEMONSTRATION SITES WHERE SCOUTING, THRESHOLDS, AND ACTIONS ARE TAKEN TO DETERMINE IF PESTICIDES ARE WARRANTED. TEN ACRES OF CORN OR SOYBEANS WILL BE USED FOR THE DEMONSTRATION ON EACH FARM. HALF THE FIELD WILL USE THE FARMER'S CURRENT PRACTICES AND HALF OF THE  FIELD WILL USE IPM PRACTICES. THESE FIELDS WILL SERVE AS EDUCATIONAL SITES WHERE FARMERS AND OTHER AGRICULTURAL INDUSTRY STAKEHOLDERS CAN LEARN ABOUT IPM TACTICS FOR SOIL PESTS THAT IMPACT CORN AND SOYBEAN.
2. THIRTY PARTNER FARMS WILL BE ACTIVE PARTICIPANTS IN THE TWO-YEAR PROJECT TO GAIN DIRECT EXPERIENCE AND ONE-ON-ONE TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE WITH SCOUTING, ACTION THRESHOLDS, AND MANAGEMENT DECISIONS. THESE FARMS WILL WORK WITH PROJECT TEAM TO SELECT AT LEAST ONE FIELD TO SCOUT, RECEIVE SCOUTING
TOOLS, AND ASSISTANCE WITH SCOUTING AND ACTION THRESHOLDS. 
3. WEBINAR SERIES - A WINTER PEST MANAGEMENT WEBINAR SERIES WILL BE HELD IN JAN/FEB OF 2022. THE WEBINAR SERIES WILL HIGHLIGHT PESTS OF CORN AND SOYBEANS, SCOUTING AND MONITORING OF THESE PESTS, ACTION THRESHOLDS, AND PEST MANAGEMENT TOOLS. ENVIRONMENTAL CONCERNS RELATED TO PESTICIDES AND OVERUSE WILL ALSO BE COVERED.
4. ON-FARM FIELD DAYS - THREE FIELD DAYS PER YEAR HELD AT DEMONSTRATION FARMS WILL PROVIDE PARTICIPANTS WITH HANDS-ON TRAINING FOR SCOUTING, IDENTIFICATION, AND ACTION THRESHOLDS.
PRESENTATION AT LOCAL AND REGIONAL WINTER CONFERENCES - NO-TILL COVER CROP CONFERENCE, GRAIN CONFERENCE, DAIRY MEETINGS, AND CERTIFIED CROP ADVISOR CONFERENCES WILL SERVE AS A PLATFORM TO DELIVER PROJECT INFORMATION TO OUR TARGET AUDIENCE. IN 2022, AN IPM INTENSIVE WILL BE HELD AT THE ANNUAL NO-TILL COVER CROP CONFERENCE TO TRAIN STAKEHOLDERS AND IN 2023 A FARMER-TO-FARMER SESSION TO HIGHLIGHT IPM OUTCOMES. SCOUTING FACTSHEETS FOR CORN AND SOYBEAN - FACTSHEETS HIGHLIGHTING PROTOCOLS, ACTION THRESHOLDS, AND MANAGEMENT OPTIONS WILL BE DEVELOPED AND POSTED ON THE WWW.UVM.EDU/NWCROPS WEBSITE.SUBRECIPIENT:NO SUBAWARDS ARE INCLUDED IN THIS ASSISTANCE AGREEMENT.OUTCOMES:DELIVERABLES:
1. CONDUCT 6 ON-FARM DEMONSTRATION SITES THAT ENGAGE KEY FARMERS IN ADOPTING IPM STRATEGIES FOR MANAGING SOIL PESTS IN CORN AND SOYBEANS IN 2022 AND 2023.
2. CONDUCT 6 ON-FARM FIELD DAYS AT THE DEMONSTRATION SITES THAT PROVIDE HANDS-ON IPM TRAINING TO FARMERS AND
AGRICULTURAL INDUSTRY STAKEHOLDERS ON IPM STRATEGIES FOR MANAGEMENT OF SOIL PESTS IN CORN AND SOYBEANS IN 2022 AND 2023.
3. COORDINATE WITH 30 PARTNER FARMS (2000 ACRES) WILL RECEIVE ONE-ON-ONE TRAINING AND TOOLS TO IMPLEMENT SCOUTING AND ACTION THRESHOLDS IN 2021 AND 2022.
4. DEVELOP AND PRESENT A 4-PART WEBINAR SERIES FOCUSED ON SOIL PESTS OF CORN AND SOYBEANS, SCOUTING, THRESHOLDS, AND IPM STRATEGIES TO REDUCE THE PROPHYLACTIC USE OF NEONICOTINOIDS IN THE WINTER OF 2022.

DESCRIPTION:INSECT PESTS REDUCE PLANT HEALTH AND MARKETABILITY, WHICH FREQUENTLY REQUIRES CHEMICAL INTERVENTION TO PRODUCE A SALEABLE PRODUCT. POLLINATORS PLAY A CRITICAL ROLE IN U.S. AGRICULTURE AND ECOSYSTEM FUNCTIONING, BUT INSECTICIDES USED DURING CROP PRODUCTION CAN HARM THEM. MOST POLLINATOR TOXICOLOGICAL DATA FOCUS ON BEES. LEPIDOPTERAN POLLINATORS (I.E., BUTTERFLIES AND MOTHS) DIFFER IN SUSCEPTIBILITY TO INSECTICIDES COMPARED TO BEES AND HAVE DIFFERENT ROUTES OF EXPOSURE (E.G., LARVAL LEAF-FEEDING VERSUS NECTAR CONSUMPTION). CONSEQUENTLY, PRODUCTS COMPATIBLE WITH BEE CONSERVATION POSE AN UNKNOWN RISK TO LEPIDOPTERAN POLLINATORS, YET BOTH TAXA ARE PERVASIVE IN AGROECOSYSTEMS. FLORIDA'S ORNAMENTAL PLANT INDUSTRY HAS AN ESTIMATED ECONOMIC IMPACT OF $21 BILLION, RANKING SECOND IN THE NATION. ORNAMENTAL PLANTS ARE A MAJOR COMPONENT OF RAPIDLY EXPANDING URBAN LANDSCAPES WHERE 80% OF THE U.S. POPULATION RESIDES, THUS MAKING PESTICIDE USE DURING PRODUCTION AND PERENNIAL MAINTENANCE RELEVANT TO MOST PEOPLE AND TO FUTURE LANDSCAPES. WE WILL DEVELOP LEPIDOPTERA IPPM STRATEGIES FOR ORNAMENTAL PLANT PRODUCTION, USE OUR RESULTS TO CREATE A WILDLIFE-FRIENDLY PLANT PRODUCTION CERTIFICATION PROGRAM, AND INCREASE THE POSITIVE ENVIRONMENTAL AND ECOLOGICAL IMPACT OF ORNAMENTAL PLANT PRODUCTION.ACTIVITIES:1. REVIEW NOVEL DATA DOCUMENTING THE ACUTE, CHRONIC, LETHAL, AND SUBLETHAL EFFECTS OF PESTICIDES TO MONARCHS OVER TIME.
2. PUBLISH IN TWO SCIENTIFIC PUBLICATIONS DISCUSSING PROJECT RESULTS AND IMPLICATIONS SUBMITTED TO AN OPEN-SOURCE, PEER REVIEWED JOURNALS.
3. DISTILL AND REVIEW ANY NEW EVIDENCE-BASED IPPM RECOMMENDATIONS FOR CONTROLLING KEY SAP-FEEDING INSECT PESTS WHILE MITIGATING IMPACTS TO LEPIDOPTERAN POLLINATORS IN A PLANT PRODUCTION ENVIRONMENT.
4. DEVELOP A COST-BENEFIT ANALYSIS TO DETERMINE ECONOMIC VIABILITY AND EFFECTIVENESS OF IPPM RECOMMENDATIONS.
5. CONCEPTUALIZE AND PRODUCE FIVE WEB-BASED TRAINING MODULES TO CERTIFY INDUSTRY PROFESSIONALS IN THE USE OF IPPM STRATEGIES TO PROTECT INSECT POLLINATORS DURING ORNAMENTAL PLANT PRODUCTION.
6. PUBLISH IN TWO INDUSTRY TRADE JOURNAL ARTICLES AND TWO EXTENSION PUBLICATIONS DISSEMINATING OUR IPPM RECOMMENDATIONS.
7. PARTICIPATE IN ONE LOCAL, ONE STATEWIDE, ONE REGIONAL, AND ONE NATIONAL TRAINING EVENT TO INCREASE AWARENESS OF IPPM PRODUCTION PRACTICES.
8. DEVELOP FINAL REPORT SUMMARIZING ALL PROJECT ACTIVITIES, DATA, PUBLICATIONS, AND QUANTIFIED OUTCOMES.SUBRECIPIENT:THE APPLICANT HAS BUDGETED A SUBCONTRACT TO UF ICBR INSECTICIDE RESIDUE ANALYSIS. IN ADDITION TO QUANTIFYING EFFECTS OF INSECTICIDES ON MONARCHS VIA EXPOSURE THROUGH HERBIVORY,THE APPLICANT WILL QUANTIFY POTENTIAL EXPOSURE TO BUTTERFLIES AND OTHER POLLINATORS VIA THE MOVEMENT OF INSECTICIDES INTO MILKWEED NECTAR. TROPICAL MILKWEED FLOWERS ARE ACTIVELY VISITED BY BEES AND BUTTERFLIES, AND EVIDENCE SUGGESTS THAT SOME SYSTEMIC INSECTICIDES CAN REDUCE MONARCH ADULT LONGEVITY VIA NECTAR FEEDING AND THUS MAY SERVE AS ANOTHER ROUTE FOR NON-TARGET INSECTICIDE IMPACTS (JAMES 2019). WE WILL OBTAIN A. CURASSAVICA PLANTS (12 PER INSECTICIDE TREATMENT) THAT ARE IN FULL BLOOM, PRUNE OFF ALL FLOWERS, MAKE FOLIAR INSECTICIDE APPLICATIONS, AND MAINTAIN THE PLANTS USING STANDARD PRODUCTION PRACTICES TO PROMOTE NEW FLOWER DEVELOPMENT. TWO WEEKS, FOUR WEEKS, AND SIX WEEKS AFTER TREATMENT, THE APPLICANT WILL COLLECT NECTAR FROM ALL PLANTS DURING THE EARLY MORNING. THE APPLICANT WILL POOL NECTAR SAMPLES FROM EACH PLANT WITHIN A TREATMENT AND PRESERVE THEM IN MICRO-CENTRIFUGE VIALS IN A -80 FREEZER UNTIL PROCESSED FOR INSECTICIDE RESIDUE ANALYSIS. THE APPLICANT WILL USE EXISTING PARTNERSHIPS WITH THE UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA INTERDISCIPLINARY CENTER FOR BIOTECHNOLOGY RESEARCH (UF ICBR) TO ANALYZE ALL NECTAR SAMPLES FOR INSECTICIDE RESIDUE CONCENTRATIONS.OUTCOMES:OBJ. 1: DETERMINE THE CHRONIC AND SUBLETHAL EFFECTS OF INSECTICIDES TO MONARCH LARVAE AND ADULTS AT APPLICATION RATES TARGETING KEY PESTS.

DESCRIPTION:THE OVERALL GOAL OF THE PROPOSED WORK IS TO INCREASE GROWER ADOPTION OF INTEGRATED PEST AND POLLINATOR MANAGEMENT TACTICS IN WATERMELON PRODUCTION. THROUGH A STATEWIDE SCOUTING PROGRAM, THE GRANTEE WILL PARTNER WITH ILLINOIS AND INDIANA GROWERS TO ADDRESS THE CHALLENGES IMPLEMENTING IPPM PRACTICES ON THEIR FARMS. IN TOTAL, THE GRANTEE EXPECTS THIS PROJECT TO IMPACT OVER 200 WATERMELON GROWERS AND THE 6,000 ACRES OF FARMLAND THEY COLLECTIVELY MANAGE. FUNDS WILL BE USED TO SUPPORT A GRADUATE STUDENT TO CONDUCT ON-FARM DEMONSTRATION TRIALS DURING THE SUMMERS OF 2022 AND 2023. IN ACCOMPLISHING THESE GOALS, THE GRANTEE ANTICIPATE REDUCING INSECTICIDE/MITICIDE APPLICATIONS IN SEEDLESS WATERMELON PRODUCTION BY 30% (2-3 SPRAYS PER SEASON) COMPARED TO CURRENT REGIMES, WHICH WILL HAVE A NUMBER OF SHORT- AND LONG-TERM BENEFITS TO GROWERS. THE GRANTEE EXPECTS DECREASED INSECTICIDE APPLICATION, INCREASED POLLINATION, AND IMPROVED FRUIT QUALITY WITH INCREASED IPPM ADOPTION IN THE SHORT-TERM. IN THE LONG-TERM, THE GRANTEE ANTICIPATES GREATER ACCESS TO PRIME MARKETS (I.E., WAL-MART'S NEW POLICY MANDATING ADOPTION OF IPM AND 'BEE-SAFE PRACTICES' BY 2025) DUE TO DECREASED PESTICIDE RESIDUES AND INCREASED VIABILITY OF AGROECOSYSTEMS THROUGH A REDUCTION IN OFF-TARGET EFFECTS FROM INSECTICIDE USE.ACTIVITIES:1. GRANTEE WILL IMPLEMENT A THRESHOLD-BASED SCOUTING PROGRAM TO INCREASE GROWER ADOPTION OF INTEGRATED PEST AND POLLINATOR MANAGEMENT PRACTICES.
A: GRANTEE CREATED AN IPPM PROGRAM WITH THE PRIMARY GOAL OF INCREASING THESE PRACTICES IN SEEDLESS WATERMELON PRODUCTION. THIS WILL REQUIRE INTENSIVE COLLABORATIONS WITH GROWERS, IN WHICH GRANTEE WILL SCOUT GROWER FIELDS AND PROVIDE WEEKLY REPORTS AND RECOMMENDATIONS FOR PRIMARY ARTHROPOD PESTS. EACH PARTICIPATING GROWER WILL SELECT ONE 20-30 ACRE WATERMELON FIELD TO BE SCOUTED WEEKLY. PLANTS WILL BE INSPECTED FOR THE PRESENCE OF COMMON ARTHROPOD PESTS. MOST PESTS WILL BE SAMPLED ON A PER PLANT BASIS (E.G., STRIPED CUCUMBER BEETLES, APHIDS, SQUASH BUGS); HOWEVER, SOME WILL BE ON A PER FLOWER OR PER LEAF BASIS. MITES WILL BE COUNTED ON THREE LEAVES PER PLANT USING A LOUPE (~240 LEAVES PER FIELD). PICKLEWORM LARVAE WILL BE SAMPLED BY PULLING 4 MALE FLOWERS PER PLANT AND EXAMINING EACH FOR ENTRY HOLES (~320 FLOWERS PER FIELD). AT THE END OF EACH GROWING SEASON, GRANTEE WILL REQUEST THAT GROWERS SUBMIT THEIR PESTICIDE RECORDS OF FIELDS SAMPLED BY THE SCOUT (I.E., PRODUCTS, RATES, DATES OF APPLICATION). PESTICIDE APPLICATION RECORDS WILL BE COMPARED WITH WEEKLY SCOUTING DATA TO DETERMINE WHETHER THE GROWER COMPLIED WITH OUR IPPM GUIDELINES (I.E., FOLLOWING THE THRESHOLD AND/OR APPLYING PRODUCTS WITH REDUCED BEE TOXICITY VALUES). GRANTEE WILL MEET WITH ALL GROWERS ON AN ANNUAL BASIS TO DISCUSS INSECTICIDE RECORDS, IN ADDITION TO WHAT STRATEGIES EITHER WORKED OR DID NOT WORK TO MANAGE THE TARGET ARTHROPOD PESTS. GROWERS WILL ALSO BE REQUIRED TO SUBMIT A SURVEY IN WHICH THEY DESCRIBE THEIR EXPERIENCES EITHER FOLLOWING OR FORGOING THE IPPM RECOMMENDATIONS.

2. GRANTEE WILL EVALUATE THE IMPACT OF IPPM PRACTICES ON WATERMELON QUALITY (POLLINATION AND INSECTICIDE RESIDUES).
A: GRANTEE WILL LEVERAGE DATA COLLECTED FROM OBJECTIVE 1. GRANTEE WILL PAIR POLLINATION OBSERVATIONS, YIELD ESTIMATES, AND RESIDUE SAMPLES TO CONDUCT POST-HOC ANALYSES AIMED AT CORRELATING THE ADOPTION OF IPPM PRACTICES WITH WATERMELON MARKETABILITY OUTCOMES. GRANTEE WILL CONDUCT POLLINATION OBSERVATIONS IN ALL TREATMENT FIELDS DURING THE BLOOM PERIOD. ALL 10 FARMS WILL BE VISITED BIWEEKLY OVER A FOUR-WEEK PERIOD (2 OBSERVATIONS PER FARM). GRANTEE WILL CONDUCT YIELD ASSESSMENTS AT TWO POINTS DURING THE GROWING SEASON, DURING BLOOM AND ONE WEEK BEFORE HARVEST. THE SIZE OF ALL MELONS WILL BE MEASURED (LENGTH AND WIDTH OF MELONS) USING A MEASURING TAPE WITHIN SAMPLING AREAS, 2M TIMES; 2M. THIS WILL BE REPLICATED 40 TIMES IN EACH FIELD. GRANTEE WILL HARVEST 20 MELONS FROM EACH FIELD TO EXTRACT INSECTICIDE RESIDUES

THESE FUNDS WILL ASSIST IN SUPPORTING THE INTEGRATED PEST MANAGEMENT (IPM) PROJECT LED BY THE UMASS CRANBERRY STATION THAT WILL EXAMINE CURRENT CRANBERRY GROWER PRACTICES TO PROMOTE TECHNIQUES THAT WILL MINIMIZE APPLICATOR EXPOSURE FROM OFF-TARGET DRIFT OR SPLASH FROM THE APPLICATION OF HERBICIDES IN CRANBERRY PRODUCTION. IN CONCERT WITH INDUSTRY PARTNERS, THIS PROJECT WILL SUPPORT FIELD WORK AND SAMPLING EFFORTS TO IMPROVE UNDERSTANDING OF RANGE OF RESIDUES (FRUIT OR LEAF TISSUES) THAT COULD OCCUR FROM POS EMERGENCE APPLICATIONS OF HERBICIDES.

DESCRIPTION:THE WORK PROPOSED BY THE SAGINAW CHIPPEWA TRIBE INVOLVES THE DEVELOPMENT OF STAFF AND MANAGEMENT EXPERTISE ON PESTICIDE PROGRAM ISSUES AND ENFORCEMENT.  SUCH ACTIVITIES INCLUDE COLLABORATIVE ATTENDANCE AND PARTICIPATION IN TRAINING OPPORTUNITIES THROUGH PREP, PIRT, FIELD-WORK EXCURSIONS, AND OTHER APPROPRIATE ACTIVITIES.  THIS WILL BE DONE THROUGH THE IMPLEMENTATION OF THE STATE/FEDERAL PESTICIDE REGULATORY AND ENFORCEMENT PROGRAMS. THIS AGREEMENT WILL RESULT IN ENHANCED PROTECTION OF HUMAN HEALTH AND THE ENVIRONMENT THROUGH THE ASSESSMENT OF PESTICIDE USE ON TRIBAL PROPERTY, EVALUATION OF COMMUNITY CONCERNS, RESPONSE TO QUESTIONS AND RESIDENTS ON TRIBAL PROPERTY, AND THE FILING OF COMPLAINTS TO EPA/MDARD AS APPROPRIATE.ACTIVITIES:THE PROGRAM ACTIVITIES ASSOCIATED WITH THIS COOPERATIVE AGREEMENT INCLUDE: (1) COMPLETING ADMINISTRATIVE/MANAGEMENT, FIDUCIARY, AND REPORTING REQUIREMENTS, (2)    PROVIDING OUTREACH, COMMUNICATION, AND TRAINING IN RESPONSE TO NEW EMERGING ISSUES, RULES, REGULATIONS, AND REGISTRATION DECISIONS, (3) REPORTING INFORMATION ON PESTICIDE INCIDENTS INVOLVING POLLINATORS TO THE ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION AGENCY (EPA) OFFICE OF PESTICIDE PROGRAMS (OPP). (4) CONDUCTING OUTREACH, EDUCATION, AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ON THE WORKER PROTECTION STANDARD (WPS) RULE'S REQUIREMENTS, WITH A FOCUS ON THE APPLICATION EXCLUSION ZONE (AEZ) AND RESPIRATOR-RELATED REQUIREMENTS, (5) SUPPORTING WPS WORKER AND HANDLER TRAINING THROUGH THE DEVELOPMENT AND DISTRIBUTION OF EPA-APPROVED TRAINING MATERIALS, FACILITATION OF TRAIN-THE-TRAINER PROGRAMS, AND UPDATING EXISTING TRAINING MATERIALS, (6) MECHANISMS AND PROCEDURES FOR COORDINATING AND FOLLOWING UP ON REPORTS OF OCCUPATIONAL PESTICIDE EXPOSURE, INCIDENTS, OR ILLNESSES, (7) ATTENDING AND PARTICIPATING IN WPS TRAINING EFFORTS INITIATED BY HQ OR EPA REGIONS, AS WELL AS OTHER RELEVANT TRAININGS, (8) PROVIDING OUTREACH, EDUCATION, AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ON CHANGES TO STATE CERTIFICATION PROGRAMS AND REQUIREMENTS RELATED TO PART 171 RULE REVISIONS, (9) IDENTIFYING PESTICIDES OF INTEREST (POI) THAT MAY THREATEN LOCAL RESOURCES OR HAVE WATER QUALITY CONCERNS IN MULTIPLE REGIONS, (10) IDENTIFYING PESTICIDES OF CONCERN (POC) BY EVALUATING POIS AND DETERMINING IF HUMAN HEALTH OR ENVIRONMENTAL REFERENCE POINTS ARE LIKELY TO BE EXCEEDED, (11) COORDINATING PREVENTION AND PROTECTION OF WATER RESOURCES WITH OTHER RELEVANT AGENCIES, (12) ESTABLISHING AND MAINTAINING RELATIONSHIPS WITH FEDERAL, STATE, TRIBAL, AND LOCAL AGENCIES, BEEKEEPER ORGANIZATIONS, GROWER ORGANIZATIONS, CROP ADVISORS, PESTICIDE MANUFACTURERS, AND OTHER STAKEHOLDER GROUPS TO SUPPORT POLLINATOR PROTECTION ACTIVITIES.SUBRECIPIENT:NO SUBAWARDS ARE INCLUDED IN THIS ASSISTANCE AGREEMENT.OUTCOMES:THIS PROJECT INVOLVES THE DEVELOPMENT OF STAFF AND MANAGEMENT EXPERTISE ON PESTICIDE PROGRAM ISSUES AND ENFORCEMENT TO BETTER SERVE TRIBAL COMMUNITIES. THROUGH THE ACQUISITION OF KNOWLEDGE AND EXPERTISE, TRIBAL STAKEHOLDERS WILL BE BETTER EQUIPPED TO IDENTIFY, ACT UPON, AND ADVOCATE FOR THE PROGRESSIVE ACTION REQUIRED TO MITIGATE THE CONSEQUENCES OF PESTICIDE USE ON AND SURROUNDING TRIBAL LANDS.

DESCRIPTION:THE AGREEMENT PROVIDES FUNDING TO THE EXTENSION FOUNDATION. SPECIFICALLY, THE RECIPIENT WILL ADMINISTER A NATIONAL SUBAWARD PROGRAM THAT ENABLES OR INCREASES THE CAPACITY OF PESTICIDE SAFETY EDUCATION PROGRAMS (PSEPS) TO DEVELOP PROGRAMS AND MATERIALS FOR PERSONS PREPARING TO BECOME PESTICIDE APPLICATORS OR MAINTAIN A CERTIFICATION. THE PURPOSE OF THIS COOPERATIVE AGREEMENT IS TO ENSURE THAT RESTRICTED USE PESTICIDES ARE USED SAFELY AND DO NOT CAUSE UNREASONABLE ADVERSE EFFECTS TO PERSONS WHO USE THEM, THE PUBLIC, OR THE ENVIRONMENT.ACTIVITIES:THE ACTIVITIES INCLUDE 1) ADMINISTERING AND CONTINUING TO IMPROVE THE NATIONAL SUBAWARD PROGRAM (APPLICATION, SUBAWARD DISBURSEMENT, REPORTING, MONITORING AND EVALUATION); 2) ENCOURAGING, FACILITATING, AND INCENTIVIZING SUBAWARD COLLABORATIONS BETWEEN LAND GRANT UNIVERSITIES AND MINORITY-SERVING INSTITUTIONS (MSIS), INCLUDING THROUGH THE SHARING AND ADAPTATION OF PESTICIDE SAFETY EDUCATION MATERIALS; 3) PENDING ANNUAL FUNDING AWARD AND AGENCY BUDGET, CONDUCTING ADDITIONAL PESTICIDE SAFETY EDUCATION ENVIRONMENTAL JUSTICE ACTIVITIES, SUCH AS AT MSIS.SUBRECIPIENT:SUBAWARDS ARE TO SO-CALLED 'PESTICIDE SAFETY EDUCATION PROGRAMS' (PSEPS) OR 'PESTICIDE SAFETY EDUCATION FUNDS MANAGEMENT PROGRAMS' (PSEFMP PROGRAMS), USUALLY LOCATED AT LAND GRANT UNIVERSITIES. SUBAWARD RECIPIENTS WILL DEVELOP OR UPDATE MATERIALS, CONDUCT OUTREACH PROGRAMS, PROVIDE TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE, AND/OR OFFER EDUCATION AND TRAINING ACTIVITIES FOR PERSONS PREPARING TO BECOME PESTICIDE APPLICATORS OR MAINTAIN A PESTICIDE APPLICATOR CERTIFICATION. PENDING ANNUAL BUDGET AND AGENCY FUNDING, SUBAWARD RECIPIENTS MAY ALSO IMPLEMENT ADDITIONAL ENVIRONMENTAL JUSTICE ACTIVITIES RELATED TO PESTICIDE SAFETY EDUCATION.OUTCOMES:THE ANTICIPATED DELIVERABLES INCLUDE 1) A USER-FRIENDLY ONLINE APPLICATION PROCESS; 2) EFFICIENT SUBRECIPIENT AWARD DISTRIBUTION; 3) AN ONLINE SUPPORT NETWORK FOR SUBRECIPIENTS; 4) A USER-FRIENDLY REPORTING SYSTEM; 5) TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE (MATERIALS, WORKSHOPS, AND MEETINGS) TO SUBRECIPIENTS; 6) EDUCATIONAL MATERIALS THAT ARE CREATED, REVIEWED, AND SHARED WITHIN THE NETWORK; 7) PROGRAM MONITORING AND EVALUATION, INCLUDING THROUGH THE ESTABLISHMENT OF AN ADVISORY COMMITTEE; 8) QUARTERLY PROGRAMMATIC REPORTS; 9) BIANNUAL FINANCIAL REPORTS, AND AN END-OF-PROJECT FINANCIAL REPORT.

THE EXPECTED OUTCOMES INCLUDE 1) INCREASED PARTICIPATION FROM MSIS AND THE NATIONWIDE LAND GRANT UNIVERSITY SYSTEM THROUGH THE STREAMLINED APPLICATION PROCESS; 2) IMPROVED CAPACITY AT EXF TO ADMINISTER THE AWARDS; 3) COLLABORATIONS BETWEEN SUBAWARD RECIPIENTS ON, AND THE SHARING AND ADAPTATION OF, PESTICIDE SAFETY EDUCATION MATERIALS FOR PESTICIDE APPLICATORS; 4) AN IMPROVED AWARD STRUCTURE AT THE END OF THE FIVE YEARS; 5) IMPROVED CAPACITY AT MSIS TO ENGAGE IN PESTICIDE SAFETY EDUCATION AND OUTREACH; AND 6) AVAILABILITY OF CULTURALLY RELEVANT EDUCATIONAL MATERIALS AND TRAINING OPPORTUNITIES TO UNDERSERVED AND MARGINALIZED POPULATIONS.

THE INTENDED BENEFICIARIES INCLUDE PEOPLE PREPARING TO BECOME PESTICIDE APPLICATORS OR MAINTAIN A CERTIFICATION.

Description:
DESCRIPTION:THE PURPOSE OF THE STATE FIFRA ISSUES RESEARCH AND EVALUATION GROUP (SFIREG) IS TO IDENTIFY, EVALUATE AND RECOMMEND SOLUTIONS TO PESTICIDE ISSUES THAT RESULT FROM THE IMPLEMENTATION OF FIFRA BY STATE REGULATORY PROGRAMS. THE OFFICE OF PESTICIDE PROGRAMS (OPP) HAS NATIONAL PROGRAM MANAGEMENT RESPONSIBILITY FOR THE FEDERAL INSECTICIDE, FUNGICIDE AND RODENTICIDE ACT (FIFRA), AND WORKS TOGETHER WITH STATES, TRIBES, AND OTHER PARTNERS, TO PROTECT PUBLIC HEALTH AND THE ENVIRONMENT BY REGULATING THE SALE AND USE OF PESTICIDES. THE SFIREG WORKING COMMITTEES INCLUDE THE PESTICIDES OPERATION AND MANAGEMENT (POM) WORKING COMMITTEE, WHICH ADDRESSES POLICY AND REGULATORY ISSUES, THE ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY ISSUES (EQI) WORKING COMMITTEE, WHICH ADDRESSES TECHNICAL AND ENVIRONMENTAL RISK ISSUES, AND WILL STAND UP A NEW ESA WORKING COMMITTEE WHICH WILL ADDRESS STATE ISSUES RELATED TO NEW ESA REGULATORY REQUIREMENTS. SFIREG WILL HOLD FOUR IN-PERSON MEETINGS EACH CALENDAR YEAR (2 FULL SFIREG MEETINGS; 2 JOINT WORKING COMMITTEE MEETINGS). THIS COOPERATIVE AGREEMENT WILL SUPPORT SFIREG ACTIVITIES, WHICH ARE CRITICAL TO MAINTAINING STRONG PESTICIDE PROGRAMS AT THE STATE AND NATIONAL LEVEL. UNDER THIS AWARD, SFIREG WILL CONTINUE ITS WORK IDENTIFYING AND EVALUATING EMERGING PESTICIDE ISSUES, AND COLLABORATING WITH EPA AND OTHER CO-REGULATORS TO ADDRESS TOPICS AS THEY ARISE. SFIREG PROVIDES STATE REGULATORY AGENCIES (SLAS) A PLATFORM FROM WHICH TO SHARE THEIR 'ON-THE-GROUND' PERSPECTIVE ON ISSUES SUCH AS CERTIFICATION AND TRAINING, WATER QUALITY, AND ENDANGERED SPECIES. THE STRONG PARTNERSHIP BETWEEN SFIREG AND EPA ENSURES THAT INFORMATION IS COMMUNICATED EFFECTIVELY AND WIDELY. THE SUCCESS OF THE NATIONAL PESTICIDE PROGRAM DEPENDS ON EFFECTIVE COMMUNICATION AND COOPERATION BETWEEN OPP AND PESTICIDE STATE LEAD AGENCIES. SFIREG ENSURES THAT THIS ESSENTIAL REGULATORY RELATIONSHIP REMAINS STRONG.ACTIVITIES:SFIREG WILL HOLD FOUR IN-PERSON MEETINGS EACH CALENDAR YEAR (2 FULL SFIREG MEETINGS; 2 JOINT WORKING COMMITTEE MEETINGS). IN ADDITION, THEY WILL STAND UP A NEW ESA WORKING COMMITTEE WHICH WILL ADDRESS STATE ISSUES RELATED TO NEW ESA REGULATORY REQUIREMENTS.

SUBRECIPIENT:NO SUBAWARDS ARE INCLUDED IN THIS ASSISTANCE AGREEMENT.OUTCOMES:DELIVERABLES INCLUDE:
1. 20 MEETINGS OVER THE FIVE-YEAR PERIOD. THE SFIREG COMMITTEE WILL HOST FOUR MEETING ANNUALLY FOR STATE LEAD AGENCIES (SLAS) AND EPA AND MAINTAIN ONGOING COMMUNICATION WITH EPA IN THE FORM OF SPECIAL PURPOSE CALLS, ISSUE PAPERS, AND COMMENTS TO THE DOCKET ON SPECIFIC PESTICIDE PROPOSED ACTIONS. 

2. ISSUE PAPERS AS NEEDED ON NATIONAL PESTICIDE ISSUES OF MUTUAL CONCERN. 

3. COMMUNICATION AS NEEDED ON TIME SENSITIVE STATE PESTICIDE CONCERNS AND CHALLENGES.

DESCRIPTION:THE OHIO DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE (ODA) CONSUMER PROTECTION LABORATORY (CPL) WILL BE HOSTING A FOUR-DAY PESTICIDE WORKSHOP FOR USEPA REGION 5 STATE LABORATORY ANALYSTS IN MARCH 2025 IN REYNOLDSBURG, OH. ENROLLMENT WILL BE OPEN TO TWO ANALYSTS FROM EACH OF THE REGION 5 STATE LABORATORIES. THIS TRAINING WILL DISCUSS LABORATORY WORK IN THE CONTEXT OF REGULATORY ACTIVITIES, ADVANCES IN CHEMICAL ANALYSIS TECHNOLOGY, AND THE USDA/AMS PESTICIDE DATA PROGRAM.ACTIVITIES:THE TRAINING WILL FOCUS ON THREE MAIN TOPICS ASSISTING IN THE ENFORCEMENT OF ENVIRONMENTAL LAWS. FIRST IS THE INTERSECTION BETWEEN REGULATORY ACTIVITIES AND LABORATORY WORK PERFORMED IN FIFRA. SECOND IS A WALKTHROUGH AND DEMONSTRATION OF THE NEW AGILENT INTUVO GC-MS SYSTEM WHICH INCLUDES HANDS-ON ACTIVITIES FOR ALL PARTICIPANTS. FINALLY, IS AN OVERVIEW OF USDA/AMS PESTICIDE DATE PROGRAM TO COMPARE HOW PRACTICES BETWEEN THE EPA AND USDA PROGRAM DIFFER AS WELL AS TAKEAWAYS THAT CAN BENEFIT BOTH PROGRAMS, INCLUDING A DISCUSSION ON STANDARD PREPARATION AND QUALITY MANAGEMENT. THE WORKSHOP WILL OCCUR OVER THE COURSE OF FOUR DAYS CONSISTING OF VENDOR PRESENTATIONS, ROUNDTABLE DISCUSSIONS, PRESENTATIONS, AND WALKTHROUGHS.  SUBRECIPIENT:NO SUBAWARDS ARE INCLUDED IN THIS ASSISTANCE AGREEMENT.OUTCOMES:GRANTEE WILL PREPARE AND SUBMIT A FINAL REPORT AFTER THE COMPLETION OF THE WORKSHOP WHICH WILL SUMMARIZE ALL WORKSHOP ACTIVITIES, FEEDBACK RECEIVED, AND SUGGESTIONS FOR FUTURE WORKSHOPS. ATTACHED TO SECTION A.23.A IS THE TENTATIVE AGENDA FOR THE WORKSHOP DETAILING THE DELIVERABLE.

Recipient: DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE FOOD & FORESTRY OKLAHOMA
Description: DESCRIPTION:THIS COOPERATIVE AGREEMENT CONTINUES THE RECIPIENT'S IMPLEMENTATION OF ITS PESTICIDE REGULATORY AND ENFORCEMENT PROGRAMS, INCLUDING, BUT NOT LIMITED TO, ACTIVITIES SUCH AS: COMPLIANCE MONITORING, ENFORCEMENT, CERTIFICATION AND TRAINING FOR PESTICIDE APPLICATORS, AND OUTREACH AND EDUCATION. THE PURPOSE OF THIS AGREEMENT IS TO SUPPORT THE RECIPIENT'S PESTICIDE PROGRAM PROMOTING ENHANCED PROTECTION OF HUMAN HEALTH AND THE ENVIRONMENT THROUGH REDUCED PESTICIDE EXPOSURE TO THE PUBLIC, AGRICULTURAL WORKERS AND HANDLERS, PROTECTION OF WATER RESOURCES FROM PESTICIDES, AND PROTECTION OF ENDANGERED SPECIES. ACTIVITIES:THIS AGREEMENT FUNDS PESTICIDE REGULATORY AND ENFORCEMENT ACTIVITIES INCLUDING: TRAINING AND CERTIFYING PESTICIDE APPLICATORS, CONDUCTING WORKER PROTECTION ACTIVITIES TO PROTECT FARMWORKERS AND COMMUNITIES, PROTECTING WATER QUALITY BY IDENTIFYING PESTICIDES OF INTEREST, IDENTIFYING LOCATIONS THAT NEED PROTECTION DUE TO THE LOCATION OF ENDANGERED SPECIES AND THEIR HABITAT, PROTECTING PUBLIC HEALTH FROM UNREGISTERED PESTICIDE PRODUCTS AND MISUSE, MONITORING FOR COMPLIANCE IN THE FIELD AND ENFORCING ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION AGENCY (EPA) PESTICIDE LABELS WHEN NEEDED, AND CONDUCTING OUTREACH AND EDUCATION TO THE REGULATED COMMUNITY AND THE PUBLIC. SUBRECIPIENT:SUBAWARD ACTIVITIES INCLUDE WORKING WITH OKLAHOMA STATE UNIVERSITY COOPERATIVE EXTENSION SERVICE'S PESTICIDE SAFETY EDUCATION PROGRAM (PSEP). PSEP WORKS WITH OKLAHOMA DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE, FOOD, AND FORESTRY (ODAFF) TO PRODUCE UPDATED PESTICIDE APPLICATOR EDUCATION MATERIALS, WHILE INCORPORATING THE MOST UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION AGENCY (EPA) RULES AND LABEL DIRECTIONS. PSEP ALSO HOSTS APPLICATOR TEST PREPARATION COURSES AND HOSTS APPLICATOR PRACTICAL EXAMS IN THEIR FACILITY FOR APPLICABLE CATEGORIES (EX: FUMIGATION). OTHER SUBAWARD ACTIVITIES INCLUDE WORKING WITH THE UNIVERSITY OF OKLAHOMA ON THE DEVELOPMENT OF A SOFTWARE APPLICATION USED BY THEIR INSPECTORS IN THE FIELD. ODAFF INSPECTORS USE THIS APPLICATION TO DOCUMENT THE LOCATIONS OF SAMPLES TAKEN (BOTH ROUTINE AND FOR-CAUSE INSPECTIONS), STORE PHOTOGRAPHS ON INSPECTION SITES, AND INCREASE THE EFFICIENCY AND GEOGRAPHICAL COVERAGE OF ROUTINE INSPECTIONS BY STORING INSPECTION LOCATIONS. THIS TECHNOLOGY ALLOWS INSPECTORS TO STORE GEOGRAPHICAL AND DOCUMENTARY INFORMATION IN REAL TIME DURING INSPECTIONS.OUTCOMES:ANTICIPATED DELIVERABLES AND EXPECTED OUTCOMES OF THIS COOPERATIVE AGREEMENT INCLUDE BETTER PROTECTION OF PUBLIC HEALTH AND THE ENVIRONMENT THROUGH 1) INCREASED OUTREACH AND EDUCATION ON PROPER PESTICIDE USE TO THE REGULATED COMMUNITY AND THE PUBLIC TO REDUCE RISK, 2) PROPER TRAINING AND CERTIFICATION OF PESTICIDE APPLICATORS, 3) MONITORING FOR COMPLIANCE BY PERFORMING INSPECTIONS, 4) RESPONDING TO TIPS AND COMPLAINTS, AND 5) TAKING ENFORCEMENT ACTION WHEN NEEDED. THE PROGRAM BENEFICIARIES ARE THE REGULATED COMMUNITY AND THE GENERAL PUBLIC.

THIS NEW COOPERATIVE AGREEMENT WILL PROVIDE THE PUBLIC WITH OBJECTIVE, SCIENCE-BASED INFORMATION ON PESTICIDE-RELATED SUBJECTS THROUGH A WEB SITE, TOLL-FREE TELEPHONE SERVICE, OUTREACH AND TRAINING. TRAINED EXPERTS WILL RESPOND TO QUESTIONS ON THE RISKS ASSOCIATED WITH PESTICIDE USE, RESTRICTIONS ON PESTICIDE USE, WHO TO CONTACT FOR REGULATORY ENFORCEMENT, HOW TO REPORT INFORMATION ON A POTENTIAL INCIDENT, AND MORE. SECONDARILY, STAFF WILL COLLECT INFORMATION ON SUSPECTED INCIDENTS VOLUNTEERED BY CALLERS.

DESCRIPTION:THE RECIPIENT, OREGON STATE UNIVERSITY (OSU), WILL ADMINISTER THE NATIONAL PESTICIDE INFORMATION CENTER (NPIC) PROVIDING THE PUBLIC WITH UNBIASED, ACCURATE, SCIENCE-BASED INFORMATION ON A BROAD RANGE OF PESTICIDE-RELATED ISSUES, IN A MANNER ACCESSIBLE TO PEOPLE WHO SPEAK DIFFERENT LANGUAGES AT VARIOUS LITERACY THROUGH A WEB SITE, TOLL-FREE TELEPHONE SERVICE, OUTREACH AND TRAINING. TRAINED EXPERTS WILL RESPOND TO QUESTIONS ON THE RISKS ASSOCIATED WITH PESTICIDE USE, RESTRICTIONS ON PESTICIDE USE, WHO TO CONTACT FOR REGULATORY ENFORCEMENT, HOW TO REPORT INFORMATION ON A POTENTIAL INCIDENT, AND MORE. SECONDARILY, STAFF WILL COLLECT INFORMATION ON SUSPECTED INCIDENTS VOLUNTEERED BY CALLERS. IN ADDITION, THE NPIC WILL PROVIDE DATA AND ANALYSIS OF INFORMATIONAL INQUIRIES AND POTENTIAL INCIDENTS, FOR STAKEHOLDERS, SUPPORTING NATIONAL PESTICIDE ACTIVITIES, INCLUDING SURVEILLANCE EFFORTS AND ENFORCEMENT PRIORITY SETTING.

WORK TO BE ACCOMPLISHED BY THIS PROJECT INCLUDE:
1. SERVE AS A SOURCE OF FACTUAL, UNBIASED INFORMATION FOR DIVERSE AUDIENCES.
2. INFORM THE PUBLIC AND PROFESSIONALS ON A WIDE VARIETY OF PESTICIDE-RELATED SUBJECTS;
3. SUPPORT EDUCATION AND OUTREACH EFFORTS BY FEDERAL, STATE, AND LOCAL AGENCIES ON PESTICIDE SAFETY TOPICS;
4. PROVIDE EDUCATION AND OUTREACH TO THE PUBLIC AND PROFESSIONALS;
5. PROVIDE ACCURATE, UNBIASED SCIENTIFIC INFORMATION IN A MANNER UNDERSTANDABLE TO A LAY AUDIENCE;
6. COLLECT AND DISSEMINATE QUALITY PESTICIDE INCIDENT DATA VIA A RIGOROUS AND WELL-DEFINED DATA COLLECTION SYSTEM; AND
7. PROVIDE EXCEPTIONAL CUSTOMER SERVICE.

WITH THIS PROJECT, EPA PARTNERS AND STAKEHOLDERS WILL ADVANCE THE PROTECTION OF HUMANS, ECOSYSTEMS, AND THE ENVIRONMENT FROM THE RISK OF PESTICIDE POISONINGS, ILLNESS, AND INJURY.



ACTIVITIES:OSU LAYS OUT A COMPREHENSIVE PLAN AND APPROACH THAT WILL ENHANCE AND EXPAND THE NPIC PROGRAM. OSU PROPOSES TO PROMOTE AND DISSEMINATE NPIC SERVICES AND OUTREACH MATERIALS VIA A DEDICATED STAFF OUTREACH COORDINATOR, AN ADVISORY BOARD, AND FORMAL AND INFORMAL PARTNERSHIPS WITH STAKEHOLDER GROUPS. THE PROPOSAL DESCRIBES HOW EACH PARTNERSHIP WILL PROMOTE AND DISSEMINATE INFORMATION TO REACH THE INTENDED AUDIENCE AND/OR POTENTIAL AUDIENCES BY UTILIZING SOCIAL MEDIA COMMUNICATIONS PLATFORMS (LIKE LIVE CHAT AND OTHER TEXT PLATFORMS) AND EVALUATING OTHER OPTIONS AND TECHNOLOGIES FOR CREATING EDUCATIONAL MATERIALS (IN MORE LANGUAGES AND LITERACY LEVELS) AND DIRECTING CAMPAIGNS TARGETING UNDERSERVED AND VULNERABLE COMMUNITIES. THIS COLLABORATION, AND IDENTIFICATION OF ROLES, WILL FOSTER MEANINGFUL INVOLVEMENT WITH UNDERREPRESENTED GROUPS AND ENSURE THAT INFORMATION REACHES TARGET AUDIENCES. IN ADDITION, THE NPIC WILL PROVIDE DATA AND ANALYSIS OF INFORMATIONAL INQUIRIES AND POTENTIAL INCIDENTS, FOR STAKEHOLDERS, SUPPORTING NATIONAL PESTICIDE ACTIVITIES, INCLUDING SURVEILLANCE EFFORTS AND ENFORCEMENT PRIORITY SETTING.
